# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 🅰️ Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define energy. List any four different forms of energy with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Energy is the capacity of a system to do work or transfer heat. It is a conserved scalar physical quantity measured in Joules (SI).

**Four forms of energy (2 marks — ½ mark each):**

1. **Kinetic Energy** — Energy of motion. Example: Wind blowing a turbine, moving water in a river.
2. **Potential Energy** — Stored energy due to position. Example: Water stored in a reservoir at height.
3. **Chemical Energy** — Energy stored in molecular bonds. Example: Coal, gasoline, food.
4. **Electrical Energy** — Energy of moving charges. Example: Current in a circuit, lightning.

*(Valid alternatives: Thermal, Nuclear, Radiant, Sound.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between renewable and non-renewable energy resources. Give two examples each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Renewable | Non-Renewable |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition (1 mark)** | Resources that are naturally replenished at a rate faster than consumption | Resources that exist in finite quantity and take millions of years to form |
| **Examples (1 mark)** | Solar energy, Wind energy, Hydel, Biomass, Tidal | Coal, Petroleum, Natural Gas, Uranium |
| **Replenish Time (1 mark)** | Continuous / Annual cycle | Geological time scales (~10⁶ years) |
| **Environmental Impact** | Low carbon footprint | High CO₂ & pollutants |
| **Availability** | Practentially inexhaustible | Limited reserves |

---

## 5.2 🅱️ Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

### ✅ **Question A (14 Marks)**
**`[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1, Q1]`**

**(a)** Explain the various forms of energy with suitable examples. Discuss the principle of energy conversion with reference to a thermal power plant. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Classify energy resources in detail. Discuss the global and Indian energy scenario with relevant statistics. **(7 marks)**

---

#### 📝 Model Solution to Q1(a) — Forms of Energy & Thermal Power Plant (7 marks)

**Introduction (1 mark):**
Energy exists in multiple interconvertible forms. The principle of energy conversion states that energy can be transformed from one form to another but the total energy remains conserved (First Law of Thermodynamics).

**Forms of Energy (4 marks — ½ mark each, 8 forms):**

1. **Mechanical Energy (KE + PE):** KE = ½mv²; PE = mgh. *Example:* Water in a dam.
2. **Thermal Energy:** Heat content of a body. *Example:* Steam from a boiler.
3. **Chemical Energy:** Stored in molecular bonds. *Example:* Coal, petroleum.
4. **Electrical Energy:** Flow of electrons. *Example:* Current in a wire.
5. **Nuclear Energy:** Binding energy of nucleus. *Example:* Fission of U-235.
6. **Radiant Energy:** Electromagnetic radiation. *Example:* Sunlight.
7. **Sound Energy:** Mechanical vibration. *Example:* Acoustic waves.
8. **Mass Energy:** $E = mc^2$. *Example:* Matter-antimatter annihilation.

**Thermal Power Plant — Energy Conversion (2 marks):**

The conversion chain is:
$$\text{Chemical (Coal)} \xrightarrow{\text{combustion}} \text{Thermal (Steam)} \xrightarrow{\text{nozzle}} \text{Kinetic (Steam jet)} \xrightarrow{\text{turbine}} \text{Mechanical (Rotation)} \xrightarrow{\text{generator}} \text{Electrical (AC)}$$

**Conclusion (½ mark):**
Every stage has thermodynamic losses. The overall plant efficiency of a coal-fired thermal plant is typically **30–40%**, with the rest lost as waste heat in condenser and flue gases.

---

#### 📝 Model Solution to Q1(b) — Classification of Energy Resources & Global/Indian Scenario (7 marks)

**A. Classification of Energy Resources (4 marks):**

**1. By Renewability (2 marks):**
- *Renewable:* Solar, Wind, Hydro, Tidal, Wave, OTEC, Biomass, Geothermal, Hydrogen.
- *Non-Renewable:* Coal, Oil, Natural Gas, Nuclear (Uranium, Thorium).

**2. By Conventional Use (1 mark):**
- *Conventional:* Coal, Oil, Gas, Firewood, Large Hydro.
- *Non-Conventional:* Solar PV, Wind, MHD, Fuel Cells, OTEC, Tidal, Biomass gasification.

**3. By Stage of Use (1 mark):**
- *Primary:* Direct from nature (coal, sun, wind, uranium).
- *Secondary:* Converted from primary (electricity, gasoline, hydrogen).

**B. Global & Indian Energy Scenario (3 marks):**

- **Global mix (2023):** Oil ~31%, Coal ~27%, Gas ~24%, Renewables ~13%, Nuclear ~4%, Hydro ~1%. (1 mark)
- **India's position:** 3rd largest energy consumer; 2nd largest importer of oil & coal. Installed capacity ~430 GW (2024), with renewables ~190 GW. (1 mark)
- **India's targets:** 500 GW non-fossil capacity by 2030; Net-zero by 2070. (1 mark)

---

### ✅ **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1, Q2]`**

**(a)** State and explain the First and Second Laws of Thermodynamics. How do they govern energy conversion processes? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hydroelectric power plant has a head of 80 m and a discharge of 50 m³/s. The overall efficiency is 85%. Calculate:
   (i) The input power available from the water.
   (ii) The electrical power output.
   (iii) The energy generated per day in kWh. **(7 marks)**

---

#### 📝 Model Solution to Q2(a) — First & Second Laws (7 marks)

**First Law of Thermodynamics (3 marks):**

*Statement:* Energy can neither be created nor destroyed; it can only be transformed from one form to another. The total energy of an isolated system is constant.

*Mathematical form:*
$$\Delta E_{system} = Q - W$$
where $Q$ = heat supplied to the system, $W$ = work done **by** the system, and $\Delta E$ = change in total energy (internal + kinetic + potential).

*Application to energy conversion:* It ensures that the **energy balance** in any conversion device (e.g., boiler, turbine, pump) is always closed. No device can produce more energy than the input.

**Second Law of Thermodynamics (3 marks):**

*Kelvin-Planck Statement:* It is impossible to construct a device operating in a cycle that produces no effect other than extracting heat from a single reservoir and converting it all to work. (i.e., **no 100% efficient heat engine**).

*Clausius Statement:* Heat cannot spontaneously flow from a colder body to a hotter body.

*Carnot Efficiency (upper limit):*
$$\eta_{Carnot} = 1 - \frac{T_C}{T_H}$$
where temperatures are in Kelvin.

*Application:* It governs the **direction** of energy flow and sets the **upper limit on efficiency** of any thermal conversion (e.g., a steam plant with $T_H = 800$ K and $T_C = 300$ K has $\eta_{Carnot} = 62.5\%$).

**Governance of Conversion Processes (1 mark):**
The First Law tells us *how much* energy we get (quantity), while the Second Law tells us *how much useful work* we can extract (quality/exergy). Together they define the **maximum possible efficiency** of any energy system.

---

#### 📝 Model Solution to Q2(b) — Numerical Problem (7 marks)

**Given:** Head $h = 80$ m, Discharge $Q = 50$ m³/s, Efficiency $\eta = 0.85$, $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s².

**(i) Input power from water (2 marks):**

Formula:
$$P_{input} = \rho g h Q$$

Substitution:
$$P_{input} = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 80 \times 50$$

Stepwise evaluation:
$$P_{input} = 1000 \times 9.81 \times 4000$$
$$P_{input} = 1000 \times 39,240$$
$$\boxed{P_{input} = 39,240,000 \text{ W} = 39.24 \text{ MW}}$$

**(ii) Electrical power output (2 marks):**

$$P_{output} = \eta \times P_{input} = 0.85 \times 39.24 \text{ MW}$$
$$\boxed{P_{output} = 33.354 \text{ MW} \approx 33.35 \text{ MW}}$$

**(iii) Energy generated per day in kWh (3 marks):**

Time per day: $t = 24$ hours.

$$E = P_{output} \times t = 33.354 \text{ MW} \times 24 \text{ h}$$

Convert MW to kW: $33.354 \text{ MW} = 33,354 \text{ kW}$.

$$E = 33,354 \text{ kW} \times 24 \text{ h}$$
$$\boxed{E = 800,496 \text{ kWh/day} \approx 8.0 \times 10^5 \text{ kWh/day}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Unit Conversion Blunder (–1 to –2 marks):** Students often leave power in **W** when the question asks for **MW or kW**. Always re-check final units.
> 2. **Forgetting efficiency (–2 marks):** In numerical problems, output = $\eta \times$ input. Skipping $\eta$ gives a wrong answer.
> 3. **Wrong temperature scale in Carnot (–2 marks):** Use **Kelvin**, not Celsius. $T(K) = T(°C) + 273$.
> 4. **Confusing definitions of "renewable" vs "non-conventional" (–1 mark):** They are NOT synonyms. **Hydro is renewable + conventional. Nuclear is non-renewable + non-conventional.**
> 5. **Skipping the "chain" in thermal plant (–1 mark):** Always write the **complete conversion chain** (Chemical → Thermal → Kinetic → Mechanical → Electrical) for full marks.
> 6. **Wrong formula for KE/PE (–1 mark):** $KE = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$ (not $mv^2$). $PE = mgh$ (not $mgh^2$).

---

## 5.3 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **📋 Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1: Introduction to Energy Conversion & Energy Resources**

- ✅ **Energy** = capacity to do work. SI unit = **Joule (J)**. 1 kWh = **3.6 × 10⁶ J**.
- ✅ **8 primary forms** of energy: Mechanical, Thermal, Chemical, Electrical, Nuclear, Radiant, Electrochemical, Mass-energy.
- ✅ **Mass-energy equivalence:** $E = mc^2$ (Einstein, 1905).
- ✅ **First Law:** Energy is conserved. $\Delta E = Q - W$.
- ✅ **Second Law:** Entropy of universe increases. **No 100% efficient heat engine.**
- ✅ **Carnot Efficiency:** $\eta = 1 - T_C/T_H$ (temperatures in **Kelvin**).
- ✅ **Renewable ≠ Non-Conventional.** Nuclear = Non-Renewable + Non-Conventional.
- ✅ **Wind power** is proportional to $v^3$ — doubling wind speed gives **8× power**.
- ✅ **Solar constant** $G_{sc} = 1367$ W/m².
- ✅ **Hydroelectric Power:** $P = \eta \rho g h Q = 9810 \, \eta \, h \, Q$ (kW, with $\rho = 1000$, $g = 9.81$).
- ✅ **Betz Limit:** Max wind turbine efficiency = **59.3%** = 16/27.
- ✅ India is the **3rd largest** energy consumer and **2nd largest** oil importer globally.
- ✅ **Thermal power plant chain:** Chemical → Thermal → Kinetic → Mechanical → Electrical.
- ✅ **PV cell** directly converts **Radiant → Electrical** (no thermal/mechanical stage).
- ✅ **Energy content of fuel** = mass × calorific value ($CV$ in J/kg or kCal/kg).
- ✅ Always quote the **First Law (conservation)** AND **Second Law (efficiency limit)** together when asked about energy conversion.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Global and Indian Scenario of Energy Resources and Consumption
Impact of Energy on Economy
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECHT614 - ENERGY ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Impact of Energy on Economy ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Impact of Energy on Economy** refers to the systematic, multi-dimensional influence that the availability, accessibility, affordability, and quality of energy resources exert on macroeconomic indicators such as Gross Domestic Product (GDP), industrial output, employment, trade balance, inflation, and overall human development indices of a nation. In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme, this concept is examined through three foundational pillars: 1. **Energy–GDP Nexus** – The empirical and theoretical relationship between national energy throughput and economic output. 2. **Energy Intensity (EI)** – A measure of the energy required to produce one unit of economic value. 3. **Energy Elasticity** – The sensitivity of energy demand relative to economic growth. > [!NOTE] > **Standard Metric (KTU Board Expectation):** The standard metric used universally to quantify this relationship is **Energy Intensity = Primary Energy Consumption (in Mtoe) ÷ GDP (in constant USD or INR)**, with a global benchmark tracked by the **International Energy Agency (IEA)**. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine the economy of a country as a **living human body**. - **Energy** is the **blood** that circulates through the body (the economy). - **Industries, transport, agriculture, and households** are the **organs** that require this blood to function. - **GDP** is analogous to the **pulse rate and overall vitality** of the body. If blood supply (energy) is cut off or becomes impure (unaffordable/unsustainable), the organs fail — factories shut down (industry collapses), trucks stop moving (transport halts), farmers cannot irrigate (agriculture drops), and people freeze or overheat (loss of welfare). The body (economy) collapses. Conversely, a healthy, oxygenated, well-circulated blood supply (clean, affordable, reliable energy) keeps every organ performing optimally, allowing the body to grow stronger, run faster, and live longer (sustainable economic growth). > [!TIP] > **Real-world example:** During the 1973 OPEC Oil Crisis, oil prices quadrupled. Countries like the USA, UK, and Japan saw their GDP growth rate drop by 2–4%, inflation soar, and unemployment rise — a textbook demonstration of energy directly damaging economic health. ### 1.3 Why This Topic Matters for KTU 2024 - It forms the **conceptual foundation** for the rest of the course (energy management, audit, conservation, renewables). - It directly maps to **CO1 (Understand the global and national energy scenario and its economic implications)**. - It is a **high-frequency short-answer and essay topic** in KTU End Semester Examinations. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Energy–Economy Feedback Loop The relationship is **bidirectional**, not one-way: **Forward Path (Energy → Economy):** - Energy availability enables industrialization → increased manufacturing output → higher GDP. - Reliable electricity supports service sector (IT, banking, telecom) → GDP growth. - Affordable cooking/lighting fuel raises human productivity → better labour output. **Feedback Path (Economy → Energy):** - Higher GDP → higher purchasing power → greater demand for vehicles, appliances, air conditioners → higher energy demand. - Economic growth requires infrastructure (roads, factories, ports) which are energy-intensive to construct. > [!NOTE] > This bidirectional causality is why energy is termed the **"master resource"** in macroeconomic literature. ### 2.2 Key Theoretical Concepts | # | Concept | Explanation | Engineering/Economic Significance | |---|---------|-------------|-----------------------------------| | 1 | **Energy Intensity (EI)** | Energy consumed per unit GDP | Indicates energy efficiency of an economy | | 2 | **Energy Elasticity (ε)** | % change in energy ÷ % change in GDP | Predicts future energy demand from GDP forecasts | | 3 | **Energy Security** | Uninterrupted availability at affordable price | Determines import-export trade balance | | 4 | **Energy Poverty** | Lack of access to modern energy services | Directly correlates with low HDI | | 5 | **Embodied Energy** | Energy used to manufacture a product | Used in life-cycle costing of goods | | 6 | **Energy Subsidy** | Government financial support to reduce energy cost | Impacts fiscal deficit and market distortion | ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Memorize the following four formulas — they appear in nearly every KTU numerical on this module.** | Formula Name | Mathematical Form | Description of Variables | Typical Units | |--------------|-------------------|--------------------------|---------------| | **Energy Intensity (EI)** | $$EI = \dfrac{E_{total}}{GDP}$$ | $E_{total}$ = total primary energy consumed; GDP = Gross Domestic Product | Mtoe per billion USD (or kgoe per USD) | | **Energy Elasticity (ε)** | $$\varepsilon = \dfrac{\% \Delta E}{\% \Delta GDP}$$ | $\%\Delta E$ = percentage change in energy; $\%\Delta GDP$ = percentage change in GDP | Dimensionless (pure number) | | **Compound Annual Growth Rate (CAGR)** | $$CAGR = \left(\dfrac{V_f}{V_i}\right)^{\frac{1}{n}} - 1$$ | $V_f$ = final value; $V_i$ = initial value; $n$ = number of years | Fraction (× 100 for %) | | **Energy Demand Forecast** | $$E_{future} = E_{present} \times (1 + \varepsilon \cdot g)^t$$ | $\varepsilon$ = elasticity; $g$ = GDP growth rate; $t$ = years into future | Same as $E_{present}$ | > [!NOTE] > **Key economic insights embedded in the formulas:** > - If $\varepsilon > 1$ → economy is **energy-intensive** (developing economies). > - If $\varepsilon < 1$ → economy is **energy-efficient** (post-industrial service economies). > - If $\varepsilon \to 0$ → economy is **decoupled** from energy (best long-term goal). ### 2.4 Energy Intensity in India: A Real-World Snapshot According to IEA data (for context only, do not cite as a specific year): - **India's EI** has been historically around **0.20 kgoe/USD** (PPP adjusted), higher than the global average of ~0.18. - This indicates India still has **significant scope for energy efficiency improvement** in industrial, agricultural, and residential sectors. ### 2.5 Real-World Utility The formulas above are used by: - **Planning Commission / NITI Aayog** of India for long-term energy demand projections. - **International Energy Agency (IEA)** in its annual *World Energy Outlook* report. - **Power utility companies** for load forecasting and capacity expansion planning. - **Corporate sustainability teams** to compute Scope 1, 2, 3 carbon footprints. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example — The "KTU Favourite" Numerical > [!NOTE] > This is the **exact type of 7-mark problem** KTU examiners love to set. Mastering this solution guarantees marks. **Problem Statement:** > A developing country had the following macroeconomic indicators: > - **GDP in 2019:** ₹ 150 lakh crore (constant 2015 prices) > - **GDP in 2024:** ₹ 195 lakh crore (constant 2015 prices) > - **Primary Energy Consumption in 2019:** 850 Mtoe > - **Primary Energy Consumption in 2024:** 970 Mtoe > > **Calculate:** > (i) Average annual GDP growth rate (CAGR) > (ii) Average annual energy consumption growth rate (CAGR) > (iii) Energy Elasticity of the country > (iv) Energy Intensity in 2019 and 2024 > (v) Comment on whether the economy is moving toward energy efficiency. --- #### Part (i): Average Annual GDP Growth Rate (CAGR) **Step 1 — Identify the variables.** $$GDP_{2019} = 150 \text{ lakh crore}, \quad GDP_{2024} = 195 \text{ lakh crore}, \quad n = 5 \text{ years}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute into the CAGR formula.** $$g_{GDP} = \left(\frac{GDP_{2024}}{GDP_{2019}}\right)^{\frac{1}{n}} - 1$$ $$g_{GDP} = \left(\frac{195}{150}\right)^{\frac{1}{5}} - 1$$ **Step 3 — Compute the ratio.** $$g_{GDP} = (1.30)^{0.2} - 1$$ **Step 4 — Evaluate the power using logarithms.** $$\ln(1.30) = 0.2624$$ $$0.2624 \times 0.2 = 0.05248$$ $$e^{0.05248} = 1.0539$$ **Step 5 — Final value.** $$g_{GDP} = 1.0539 - 1 = 0.0539$$ **Step 6 — Express as a percentage.** $$\boxed{g_{GDP} \approx 5.39\% \text{ per annum}}$$ --- #### Part (ii): Average Annual Energy Consumption Growth Rate (CAGR) **Step 1 — Identify variables.** $$E_{2019} = 850 \text{ Mtoe}, \quad E_{2024} = 970 \text{ Mtoe}, \quad n = 5 \text{ years}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute into the CAGR formula.** $$g_{E} = \left(\frac{E_{2024}}{E_{2019}}\right)^{\frac{1}{n}} - 1$$ $$g_{E} = \left(\frac{970}{850}\right)^{\frac{1}{5}} - 1$$ $$g_{E} = (1.1412)^{0.2} - 1$$ **Step 3 — Compute logarithmically.** $$\ln(1.1412) = 0.1320$$ $$0.1320 \times 0.2 = 0.0264$$ $$e^{0.0264} = 1.0268$$ **Step 4 — Final value.** $$g_{E} = 1.0268 - 1 = 0.0268$$ **Step 5 — Express as a percentage.** $$\boxed{g_{E} \approx 2.68\% \text{ per annum}}$$ --- #### Part (iii): Energy Elasticity (ε) **Step 1 — Compute total percentage change in GDP over 5 years.** $$\%\Delta GDP = \frac{GDP_{2024} - GDP_{2019}}{GDP_{2019}} \times 100$$ $$\%\Delta GDP = \frac{195 - 150}{150} \times 100 = 30.00\%$$ **Step 2 — Compute total percentage change in Energy over 5 years.** $$\%\Delta E = \frac{E_{2024} - E_{2019}}{E_{2019}} \times 100$$ $$\%\Delta E = \frac{970 - 850}{850} \times 100 = 14.12\%$$ **Step 3 — Apply the elasticity formula.** $$\varepsilon = \frac{\%\Delta E}{\%\Delta GDP} = \frac{14.12}{30.00}$$ $$\boxed{\varepsilon \approx 0.47}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Alternative cross-check using growth rates:** $\varepsilon \approx g_E / g_{GDP} = 2.68 / 5.39 = 0.497$ — both methods give consistent values in the **0.45–0.50** range. The small difference arises from the choice of average vs total growth basis. --- #### Part (iv): Energy Intensity (EI) for 2019 and 2024 **Step 1 — Compute EI for 2019.** $$EI_{2019} = \frac{E_{2019}}{GDP_{2019}} = \frac{850 \text{ Mtoe}}{150 \text{ lakh crore}}$$ $$EI_{2019} = 5.667 \text{ Mtoe per lakh crore}$$ **Step 2 — Compute EI for 2024.** $$EI_{2024} = \frac{E_{2024}}{GDP_{2024}} = \frac{970 \text{ Mtoe}}{195 \text{ lakh crore}}$$ $$EI_{2024} = 4.974 \text{ Mtoe per lakh crore}$$ --- #### Part (v): Engineering Interpretation / Comment | Parameter | 2019 | 2024 | Trend | |-----------|------|------|-------| | GDP (lakh crore ₹) | 150 | 195 | ↑ +30% | | Energy (Mtoe) | 850 | 970 | ↑ +14.12% | | Energy Intensity | 5.667 | 4.974 | ↓ −12.2% | | Energy Elasticity | — | 0.47 | < 1 | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Examiner-ready comments (write at least 3 lines in the exam):** > 1. The economy is growing at **5.39% per annum** while energy is growing only at **2.68% per annum** → the country is **partially decoupled** from energy. > 2. Since $\varepsilon = 0.47 < 1$, the economy is **moving toward energy efficiency**. > 3. Energy intensity has **dropped by 12.2%** in 5 years, indicating successful adoption of efficient technologies, structural shift from industry to services, and/or policy interventions. > 4. To continue this trend, the country should invest in **renewables, energy-efficient industrial processes, and demand-side management**. ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Quick Decision Framework | Observation | Diagnosed State | Recommended Policy | |-------------|-----------------|--------------------| | $\varepsilon > 1.2$ | Energy-intensive, vulnerable economy | Mandate energy audits, industrial retrofitting | | $0.8 < \varepsilon < 1.2$ | Normal growth phase | Continue current mix, plan for grid expansion | | $\varepsilon < 0.8$ | Efficient, mature economy | Push for renewables, electrification of transport | | $\varepsilon \to 0$ | Fully decoupled (rare) | Maintain efficiency standards; export the model | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram: The Energy–Economy Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Primary Energy Sources
Coal Oil Gas Nuclear Hydro Solar Wind] --> B[Energy Conversion
Power Plants Refineries] B --> C[Energy Distribution
Grid Pipelines] C --> D[End-Use Sectors
Industry Transport Agriculture Residential Commercial] D --> E[Economic Output
GDP Industrial Output Employment Exports] E --> F[Macroeconomic Indicators
GDP per Capita HDI Trade Balance Inflation] F --> G[Policy and Investment Decisions] G --> A D -.Feedback.-> A E -.Feedback.-> B ``` ### 4.2 Diagram: Energy Elasticity Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Compute Energy Elasticity epsilon] --> B{epsilon value} B -- Greater than 1.2 --> C[Energy Intensive Economy] B -- Between 0.8 and 1.2 --> D[Normal Coupling] B -- Less than 0.8 --> E[Efficient Economy] B -- Approximately 0 --> F[Fully Decoupled Economy] C --> G[Action: Mandate efficiency programs] D --> H[Action: Balanced energy mix planning] E --> I[Action: Promote renewables and EVs] F --> J[Action: Maintain standards and export know-how] ``` ### 4.3 Diagram: Components of Energy Intensity Reduction ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Energy Intensity Reduction Pathways] --> B[Technological Pathway] A --> C[Structural Pathway] A --> D[Behavioral Pathway] B --> B1[Efficient Motors] B --> B2[LED Lighting] B --> B3[High-Efficiency Boilers] B --> B4[Smart Grids] C --> C1[Shift from Industry to Services] C --> C2[Move from Heavy to Light Manufacturing] C --> C3[Digitalization of Processes] D --> D1[Public Awareness] D --> D2[Energy Labels] D --> D3[Behavioral Nudges and Tariffs] ``` > [!TIP] > **KTU Exam Tip:** Drawing such simple **block diagrams** (3–5 boxes with arrows) for an essay-type answer can fetch you **1–2 extra marks** for visual presentation, as recommended by KTU's OBE (Outcome-Based Education) framework. ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam – May 2023] **Define Energy Intensity. Mention its units and explain its significance as a macroeconomic indicator.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Definition (1.5 marks):** Energy Intensity is defined as the amount of primary energy consumed per unit of Gross Domestic Product (GDP). Mathematically, > $$EI = \frac{\text{Total Primary Energy Consumption}}{\text{GDP}}$$ > > **Units (0.5 mark):** Mtoe per billion USD (in international comparisons) or kgoe per USD in PPP terms. > > **Significance (1 mark):** A lower EI indicates a more energy-efficient economy. Countries with high EI (e.g., China, Russia) are considered energy-intensive, while those with low EI (e.g., Japan, UK, Switzerland) have more efficient, service-oriented economies. EI is used by IEA and World Bank to rank countries and track sustainability progress. #### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022] **What is meant by Energy Elasticity? Mention its typical range for a developing economy.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Definition (1.5 marks):** Energy Elasticity ($\varepsilon$) is the ratio of the percentage change in energy demand to the percentage change in GDP. > $$\varepsilon = \frac{\%\Delta \text{Energy}}{\%\Delta \text{GDP}}$$ > > **Typical Range (1 mark):** For a developing economy, $\varepsilon$ is typically between **0.8 and 1.2** (sometimes up to 1.5 in early industrial phases). For a developed, post-industrial economy, $\varepsilon$ falls to **0.3–0.6**. > > **Interpretation (0.5 mark):** $\varepsilon > 1$ means economic growth is leading to disproportionately higher energy demand — a sign of an energy-intensive economy. $\varepsilon < 1$ indicates partial decoupling of growth from energy consumption. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Essay / Numerical Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!IMPORTANT] > Per KTU 2024 Scheme End Semester Examination pattern, every Module-1 question for 14 marks carries an **internal choice** between two alternatives. Both are provided below. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style] **(a)** Discuss in detail the relationship between energy consumption and economic development. Highlight the role of energy in industrialisation, agriculture, and the service sector. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With the help of a suitable example, explain how fluctuations in energy prices impact the macroeconomic indicators of a country. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **(a) Energy Consumption and Economic Development (7 marks):** 1. **Industrialization (2.5 marks):** Energy is the backbone of industrial production. Steel, cement, chemicals, textiles, and manufacturing sectors require uninterrupted electrical and thermal energy. Historical evidence shows that during the Industrial Revolution, the UK consumed 1 million tons of coal in 1800; by 1900, it had risen to 250 million tons, coinciding with its economic dominance. A 10% rise in industrial energy supply typically correlates with a 6–8% rise in industrial output in developing economies. 2. **Agriculture (2 marks):** Mechanized farming, irrigation pumps, fertilizer production, cold storage, and food processing all require energy. India's Green Revolution (1960s–70s) was powered by subsidized electricity for tube wells, raising food grain output from 72 million tonnes (1965) to 176 million tonnes (1990). Energy access transforms subsistence agriculture into commercial agriculture. 3. **Service Sector (2.5 marks):** The modern service economy — IT, banking, telecommunications, healthcare, education — depends critically on **reliable electricity**. India's IT-BPM sector, contributing ~7% of GDP, requires 24×7 power. According to industry estimates, a 1% rise in power outage frequency reduces IT firm productivity by ~0.3%. Hence, energy quality (not just quantity) is essential for the tertiary sector. --- **(b) Impact of Energy Price Fluctuations (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Direct Cost-Push Inflation (2.5 marks):** A rise in crude oil price raises the cost of transportation, manufacturing, and feedstock for petrochemicals. This propagates through the economy as cost-push inflation. Example: When global crude oil prices rose from $30/barrel (2014) to $115/barrel (June 2022), India's WPI inflation rose to 15.88% in May 2022. **Step 2 — Trade Balance Deterioration (2 marks):** India imports ~85% of its crude oil requirement. A $10/barrel rise increases the import bill by ~$15 billion annually, widening the Current Account Deficit (CAD) and putting downward pressure on the rupee. **Step 3 — Reduced Consumer Purchasing Power (1.5 marks):** Higher fuel and electricity tariffs reduce disposable income, suppressing demand for non-essential goods, slowing GDP growth. **Step 4 — Monetary Policy Response (1 mark):** Persistent energy-driven inflation forces the central bank to raise interest rates, which in turn reduces investment and slows capital formation. > [!NOTE] > **Conclusion (must be written to fetch full marks):** Energy price stability is a precondition for macroeconomic stability. Countries that diversify their energy mix (renewables + nuclear + domestic gas) and build strategic petroleum reserves are better insulated from external price shocks. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** [Alternative Choice – Internal Choice] **(a)** Define Energy Elasticity. A country's GDP grew from ₹ 100 lakh crore to ₹ 125 lakh crore over 5 years, while its energy consumption increased from 800 Mtoe to 920 Mtoe. Calculate the energy elasticity and interpret the result. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss the role of renewable energy in promoting sustainable economic development. List at least four techno-economic benefits. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer:** **(a) Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Compute % change in GDP.** $$\%\Delta GDP = \frac{125 - 100}{100} \times 100 = 25\%$$ **Step 2 — Compute % change in Energy.** $$\%\Delta E = \frac{920 - 800}{800} \times 100 = 15\%$$ **Step 3 — Apply elasticity formula.** $$\varepsilon = \frac{\%\Delta E}{\%\Delta GDP} = \frac{15}{25} = 0.60$$ **Step 4 — Interpretation (must write 3 lines for full marks).** Since $\varepsilon = 0.60 < 1$, the country exhibits **partial decoupling** of energy consumption from economic growth. For every 1% increase in GDP, energy demand rises by only 0.6%, indicating a moderately energy-efficient economy. To achieve full decoupling, the country must invest in renewables, energy-efficient technologies, and structural shifts toward the service sector. > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake to Avoid:** Many students write the inverse ratio ($\%\Delta GDP / \%\Delta E$) — that is wrong! Elasticity is always $\%\Delta E \div \%\Delta GDP$. **Memorize the order: Energy in numerator, GDP in denominator.** --- **(b) Role of Renewable Energy in Sustainable Economic Development (7 marks):** 1. **Employment Generation (2 marks):** The renewable energy sector is more labour-intensive per MW installed compared to fossil fuels. IRENA estimates that renewable energy jobs globally have grown to over 13 million, with solar PV being the largest employer. 2. **Energy Security (1.5 marks):** Domestic renewable resources (sun, wind, biomass) reduce dependence on imported fuels, improving the trade balance and insulating the country from global price volatility. 3. **Decentralized Power (1.5 marks):** Solar mini-grids and rooftop systems can electrify remote rural areas, boosting agricultural productivity, small-scale industries, and educational outcomes. 4. **Environmental Co-Benefits (1.5 marks):** Renewables reduce CO₂, SO₂, and particulate emissions, lowering healthcare costs and avoiding climate-related economic losses. India has committed to 500 GW of non-fossil capacity by 2030, supporting its net-zero 2070 target. 5. **Foreign Investment Attraction (0.5 mark):** Green Hydrogen and offshore wind attract ESG (Environmental, Social, Governance) capital from international markets. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Where students typically lose marks on this topic:** > 1. **Formula Confusion (–1 to –2 marks):** Mixing up Energy Intensity and Energy Elasticity formulas. **Remember:** Intensity uses **absolute values** (E/GDP), Elasticity uses **percentage changes** (%ΔE / %ΔGDP). > 2. **No Units Mentioned (–0.5 to –1 mark):** Always specify units (Mtoe, kgoe, billion USD). KTU examiners explicitly award a unit-mark in numericals. > 3. **CAGR Misuse (–1 mark):** CAGR and simple average growth are different. Use CAGR for multi-year data. > 4. **Lack of Interpretation (–1 to –2 marks):** KTU 2024 Scheme values "analysis and interpretation" highly. After every numerical, write **at least 3 lines** of interpretation in plain English. > 5. **No Diagrams (–0.5 mark):** Adding a small block diagram or pie chart shows OBE-aligned holistic understanding. > 6. **Missing Conclusion (–0.5 mark):** Always end essay-type answers with a 1–2 line summary/conclusion. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid Revision Checklist (Read 2 hours before the exam):** - ✅ **Energy Intensity** = Total Energy ÷ GDP. Lower EI = more efficient economy. - ✅ **Energy Elasticity** = %ΔE ÷ %ΔGDP. If ε < 1, economy is partially decoupled. - ✅ **CAGR formula** is the most-tested numerical tool in this module. - ✅ **Four major macro-indicators affected by energy:** GDP, Inflation, Trade Balance, Employment. - ✅ **Energy–Economy relationship is bidirectional:** Energy enables growth; growth drives energy demand. - ✅ **Three pillars of energy's economic impact:** Availability, Affordability, Reliability. - ✅ **Energy Security** = Uninterrupted supply at affordable price, free from geopolitical disruption. - ✅ **India's EI** is higher than the global average → significant scope for efficiency improvement. - ✅ **OPEC 1973, 1979, 1990 Gulf War, 2022 Russia-Ukraine** are key historical events to cite for energy-economy linkage. - ✅ **Renewables** = jobs + energy security + decarbonization + rural electrification. - ✅ Always write **units, formulae, and interpretation** — not just final answers. - ✅ For 14-mark answers, follow the structure: **Definition → Explanation → Example → Diagram → Conclusion**. > **End of Module 1 Notes — Impact of Energy on Economy**

topics.title: Conventional Energy Conversion Plants - Thermal, Hydro, and Nuclear Power Plants
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT614 - ENERGY ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Conventional Energy Conversion Plants - Thermal, Hydro, and Nuclear Power Plants

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Conventional Energy Conversion Plants — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> *Conventional Energy Conversion Plants* are large-scale, grid-connected electricity generation stations that convert the **chemical energy of fossil fuels**, the **gravitational potential energy of water**, or the **nuclear binding energy of fissile isotopes** into electrical energy through well-established thermodynamic cycles (Rankine, Brayton, or indirect fission–steam cycles) and electromechanical generators.

These three families of plants form the **backbone of global baseload electricity generation** and are classified as *conventional* because they rely on well-understood, mature, and commercially deployed technologies rather than emerging renewable mechanisms.

### The Three Pillars of Conventional Generation

| Plant Type | Primary Energy Source | Working Fluid / Cycle | Typical Conversion Efficiency |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Thermal Power Plant** | Coal / Oil / Natural Gas | Steam (Rankine) / Gas (Brayton) | **35 % – 45 %** |
| **Hydro Power Plant** | Stored Water at Elevation | Water (Hydraulic turbine) | **85 % – 92 %** |
| **Nuclear Power Plant** | U-235 / Pu-239 Fission | Pressurized Steam (Rankine) | **32 % – 38 %** |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Three Ways to Push a Turbine" Analogy**
> Imagine you have a giant fan blade (turbine) and you need to spin it to make electricity. There are three classic ways:
>
> 1. **Boil water into high-pressure steam and blow it at the blade** → *Thermal Power Plant* (uses coal/gas as the heat source).
> 2. **Drop a heavy bucket of water from a great height onto the blade** → *Hydro Power Plant* (uses gravity of stored water).
> 3. **Boil water using the heat released from splitting atoms** → *Nuclear Power Plant* (uses nuclear fission as the heat source).
>
> In every case, the *spinning turbine* is what ultimately drives the generator that produces electricity. Only the **source of the rotational push** differs.

### Real-World Significance for a KTU Engineer

- **Thermal Plants** currently account for roughly **57 % of global electricity** generation.
- **Hydro** provides the **largest share of renewable electricity worldwide** at ~ 15 %.
- **Nuclear** supplies about **10 % of global electricity** but with the lowest carbon footprint per kWh among conventional sources (~ **12 g CO₂/kWh**, compared to ~ **820 g CO₂/kWh** for coal).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Engineering Constants to Memorize:**
> - Standard gravitational acceleration: **g = 9.81 m/s²**
> - Density of water: **ρ = 1000 kg/m³**
> - 1 calorie = **4.186 J**
> - 1 kWh = **3.6 × 10⁶ J**
> - 1 MW = **10⁶ W**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Thermal Power Plant (Steam / Rankine-Based)

### 2.1.1 Major Subsystems

A modern coal-fired thermal power plant is organized into **four functional blocks**:

1. **Coal Handling & Preparation Plant (CHPP)** — receives, crushes, and pulverizes coal.
2. **Boiler / Steam Generator** — burns fuel to convert water into superheated steam.
3. **Turbine–Generator Set** — high-pressure (HP), intermediate-pressure (IP), and low-pressure (LP) turbines coupled to an alternator.
4. **Condenser & Cooling System** — condenses exhaust steam back to water for reuse.

Auxiliaries include electrostatic precipitators (ESP), economizers, superheaters, deaerators, and ash handling systems.

### 2.1.2 Working Principle (Why and How)

The plant operates on the **Rankine Cycle**:

- **Why?** It is a *closed* vapor power cycle that recycles the working fluid (water), making it practical for continuous operation.
- **How?** Water → pressurized, heated, vaporized, superheated → expanded through turbine (work extracted) → condensed → pumped back to boiler.

The four ideal Rankine processes are:

| Process | Component | Energy Transfer |
|---|---|---|
| 1 → 2 | Pump | Compresses liquid (small work input) |
| 2 → 3 | Boiler | Heat added at constant pressure |
| 3 → 4 | Turbine | Isentropic expansion (work output) |
| 4 → 1 | Condenser | Heat rejected at constant pressure |

---

## 2.2 Hydro Power Plant

### 2.2.1 Classification by Head

| Plant Class | Head Range (m) | Turbine Used |
|---|---|---|
| **Low Head** | < 30 m | Kaplan / Propeller |
| **Medium Head** | 30 m – 300 m | Francis (Reaction) |
| **High Head** | > 300 m | Pelton (Impulse) |

### 2.2.2 Site Selection Criteria (KTU High-Yield)

- **Hydrology:** adequate and perennial water flow (Q in m³/s).
- **Topography:** suitable dam site (narrow gorge with strong abutments).
- **Head (H):** maximum vertical drop achievable.
- **Geology:** sound rock foundation; minimal seismic risk.
- **Environmental & social impact:** displacement, submergence of forest/agricultural land.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Hydro is the "Premium Renewable":**
> Hydro plants offer the **highest conversion efficiency (≈ 90 %)** of any large-scale generation technology, **zero fuel cost**, very **long life (50–100 years)**, and provide **black-start capability** (ability to start without external power) — a critical grid-stability feature.

---

## 2.3 Nuclear Power Plant

### 2.3.1 Core Components

1. **Reactor Core** — contains fuel rods (UO₂ pellets in Zircaloy cladding), moderator (light water / heavy water / graphite), and control rods (B₄C or Cd).
2. **Moderator** — slows fast neutrons to thermal energies (~ 0.025 eV) for sustained fission.
3. **Control Rods** — absorb neutrons to regulate chain reaction rate.
4. **Primary Coolant Loop** — transfers fission heat to the steam generator.
5. **Steam Generator** — secondary loop where heat produces steam that drives the turbine.
6. **Containment Structure** — thick reinforced-concrete dome that prevents release of radioactive material.

### 2.3.2 Fission Physics — The "Why"

$$^{235}_{92}\text{U} + ^{1}_{0}\text{n} \longrightarrow ^{141}_{56}\text{Ba} + ^{92}_{36}\text{Kr} + 3\,^{1}_{0}\text{n} + \text{Energy} (\approx 200 \text{ MeV})$$

- **Why?** Each fission releases ~ **200 MeV** of energy because the total binding energy of the fission fragments is greater than that of the original U-235 nucleus.
- **How?** The released neutrons trigger further fissions — a self-sustaining **chain reaction** with multiplication factor **k ≈ 1** (critical).

> [!NOTE]
> **Energy Distribution per Fission (Approximate):**
> - Kinetic energy of fragments ≈ **165 MeV**
> - Neutron kinetic energy ≈ **5 MeV**
> - Gamma rays / β decay ≈ **15 MeV**
> - Neutrinos (lost) ≈ **10 MeV**
> - Recoverable in reactor ≈ **190 MeV**

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\eta_{\text{Rankine}} = \dfrac{W_{\text{net}}}{Q_{\text{in}}} = \dfrac{(h_3 - h_4) - (h_2 - h_1)}{h_3 - h_2}$ | Thermal efficiency of Rankine cycle | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - \dfrac{T_L}{T_H}$ | Maximum theoretical efficiency | dimensionless |
| 3 | $P_{\text{hydro}} = \eta \, \rho \, g \, Q \, H$ | Electrical power output of hydro plant | W (watts) |
| 4 | $E_{\text{fission}} = \Delta m \, c^2$ | Energy released per fission event | J |
| 5 | $\text{Plant Load Factor (PLF)} = \dfrac{\text{Energy generated (kWh)}}{\text{Installed capacity (kW)} \times \text{Hours in period}} \times 100$ | Capacity utilization indicator | % |
| 6 | $\text{Capacity Factor (CF)} = \dfrac{\text{Average load}}{\text{Rated capacity}} \times 100$ | Long-term utilization | % |
| 7 | $\text{Heat Rate (HR)} = \dfrac{Q_{\text{in}}}{\text{Energy output}} = \dfrac{3600}{\eta} \times 1000$ | Fuel input per unit electricity | kJ/kWh |
| 8 | $\text{Specific Fuel Consumption (SFC)} = \dfrac{\text{Fuel burnt}}{\text{Energy generated}}$ | Mass of fuel per kWh | kg/kWh |
| 9 | $Q = \dfrac{A \, V}{t}$ | Discharge = Area × Velocity × time factor | m³/s |
| 10 | $\eta_{\text{overall}} = \eta_{\text{thermal}} \times \eta_{\text{mechanical}} \times \eta_{\text{electrical}}$ | Combined plant efficiency | dimensionless |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical KTU Exam Pitfall:** When using $P = \eta \rho g Q H$, students often forget that $Q$ is in **m³/s** and $H$ is in **metres**. Do not substitute litres or centimetres. Always convert to SI base units first.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Thermal:** India's *NTPC Vindhyachal Super Thermal Power Station* (4760 MW) — one of the largest in India.
- **Hydro:** *Tehri Dam* (2400 MW) in Uttarakhand — tallest dam in India (260.5 m).
- **Nuclear:** *Kudankulam Nuclear Power Plant* (2000 MW) — Indo-Russian VVER reactors in Tamil Nadu.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Rankine Cycle Thermal Efficiency

The **thermal efficiency** of a steam power plant is the ratio of net work output to the heat supplied in the boiler. We derive it from first principles using enthalpy values at each state point.

### Step 1 — Identify the Four States

- State 1: Saturated liquid leaving condenser
- State 2: Compressed liquid leaving pump
- State 3: Superheated steam leaving boiler
- State 4: Wet steam leaving turbine (after isentropic expansion)

### Step 2 — Write the Energy Balances for Each Component

**Pump work input (per kg of working fluid):**

$$w_{\text{pump}} = h_2 - h_1 = v_1 (P_2 - P_1)$$

where $v_1$ is the specific volume of saturated liquid at condenser pressure.

**Boiler heat input:**

$$q_{\text{in}} = h_3 - h_2$$

**Turbine work output:**

$$w_{\text{turbine}} = h_3 - h_4$$

**Condenser heat rejection:**

$$q_{\text{out}} = h_4 - h_1$$

### Step 3 — Compute Net Work Output

$$W_{\text{net}} = w_{\text{turbine}} - w_{\text{pump}} = (h_3 - h_4) - (h_2 - h_1)$$

### Step 4 — Form the Efficiency Ratio

$$\eta_{\text{th}} = \dfrac{W_{\text{net}}}{q_{\text{in}}} = \dfrac{(h_3 - h_4) - (h_2 - h_1)}{h_3 - h_2}$$

### Step 5 — Practical Simplification

Since the pump work is small compared to turbine work (typically < 2 %), an **approximate Rankine efficiency** is often used:

$$\eta_{\text{th}} \approx \dfrac{h_3 - h_4}{h_3 - h_2}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** Increasing either the boiler pressure $P_2$ or the superheat temperature $T_3$ shifts the cycle toward higher mean temperature of heat addition, raising efficiency — but is constrained by metallurgy (creep limits of superalloys).

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Electrical Power Output of a Hydroelectric Plant

### Step 1 — Available Hydraulic Power

Consider a mass flow rate $\dot{m}$ of water falling through a net head $H$. The gravitational potential energy released per second is:

$$P_{\text{hydraulic}} = \dot{m} \, g \, H$$

### Step 2 — Express Mass Flow in Terms of Volume Flow

$$\dot{m} = \rho \, Q$$

where $Q$ is the volumetric discharge in m³/s.

### Step 3 — Substitute into Power Equation

$$P_{\text{hydraulic}} = \rho \, Q \, g \, H$$

### Step 4 — Apply Turbine–Generator Efficiency

The turbine converts hydraulic energy to mechanical work with efficiency $\eta_t$, and the generator converts mechanical to electrical with efficiency $\eta_g$. The combined overall efficiency is $\eta = \eta_t \times \eta_g$.

$$P_{\text{electrical}} = \eta \, \rho \, Q \, g \, H$$

### Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example

> A hydro station has $\eta = 0.88$, $Q = 50 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$, $H = 60 \text{ m}$. Compute $P_{\text{electrical}}$ in MW.

**Given:**

$$\eta = 0.88,\quad \rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3,\quad g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2,\quad Q = 50 \text{ m}^3/\text{s},\quad H = 60 \text{ m}$$

**Substitute:**

$$P_{\text{electrical}} = 0.88 \times 1000 \times 50 \times 9.81 \times 60$$

**Step-by-step multiplication:**

$$1000 \times 50 = 50{,}000$$

$$50{,}000 \times 9.81 = 490{,}500$$

$$490{,}500 \times 60 = 29{,}430{,}000$$

$$29{,}430{,}000 \times 0.88 = 25{,}898{,}400 \text{ W}$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{P_{\text{electrical}} \approx 25.9 \text{ MW}}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Energy Released per U-235 Fission

### Step 1 — Apply Mass–Energy Equivalence

$$E = \Delta m \, c^2$$

where $\Delta m$ = mass defect between reactants and products, $c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$.

### Step 2 — Determine the Mass Defect

For one fission event of U-235 (using approximation):

- Mass of reactants (U-235 + 1 neutron) ≈ **236.053 u**
- Mass of products (typical Ba-141 + Kr-92 + 3n) ≈ **235.868 u**
- Mass defect $\Delta m \approx 0.185 \text{ u}$

### Step 3 — Convert Atomic Mass Units to kg

$$1 \text{ u} = 1.6605 \times 10^{-27} \text{ kg}$$

$$\Delta m = 0.185 \times 1.6605 \times 10^{-27} = 3.072 \times 10^{-28} \text{ kg}$$

### Step 4 — Compute Released Energy

$$E = 3.072 \times 10^{-28} \times (3 \times 10^8)^2$$

$$E = 3.072 \times 10^{-28} \times 9 \times 10^{16}$$

$$E = 27.65 \times 10^{-12} \text{ J} = 2.765 \times 10^{-11} \text{ J}$$

### Step 5 — Convert to MeV

$$E = \dfrac{2.765 \times 10^{-11}}{1.602 \times 10^{-13}} \approx 172.6 \text{ MeV}$$

(The standard accepted value is **~ 200 MeV** because decay of fission products continues to release energy after the initial event.)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sanity Check:** 1 kg of U-235 completely fissioned releases ≈ **8.2 × 10¹³ J**, equivalent to the energy from burning **~ 2.7 million kg of coal**. This shows why nuclear fuel has an extraordinarily high energy density.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Plant Selection

| Criterion | Thermal (Coal) | Hydro | Nuclear |
|---|---|---|---|
| Initial Capital Cost | High | Very High | Very High |
| Fuel Cost | Moderate | **Zero** | Low |
| Operating Cost | High | Low | Moderate |
| Construction Time | 4 – 6 yrs | 6 – 10 yrs | 6 – 12 yrs |
| Plant Life | 25 – 30 yrs | **50 – 100 yrs** | 40 – 60 yrs |
| Capacity Factor | 60 – 75 % | 30 – 50 % (run-of-river) / **90 % (storage)** | **85 – 95 %** |
| CO₂ Emissions (g/kWh) | ~ 820 | ~ 4 | ~ 12 |
| Start-up Time | Hours | Seconds (Peaking) | Days |
| Land Footprint | Large | Very Large (reservoir) | Small |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Thermal Power Plant Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Coal Yard / Fuel Storage] --> B[Coal Handling Plant]
    B --> C[Pulverizer]
    C --> D[Boiler / Steam Generator]
    D -->|Superheated Steam HP| E[High Pressure Turbine]
    E --> F[Intermediate Pressure Turbine]
    F --> G[Low Pressure Turbine]
    G -->|Exhaust Steam| H[Condenser]
    H -->|Condensate| I[Feedwater Pump]
    I --> D
    G --> J[AC Alternator / Generator]
    J -->|Step-up| K[Power Transformer]
    K --> L[Switchyard / Grid]
    D -->|Flue Gas| M[Electrostatic Precipitator]
    M --> N[Stack to Atmosphere]
```

> **Reading Guide:** Fuel enters at node A, becomes steam in D, spins the turbines E–F–G, drives the generator J, and exits to the grid at L. The water loop (H–I–D) closes the Rankine cycle. Pollution control happens at M–N.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Hydro Power Plant Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[Reservoir] -->|Penstock| T1[Turbine]
    T1 --> G1[Synchronous Generator]
    G1 --> EX1[Exciter System]
    G1 --> TR1[Step-up Transformer]
    TR1 --> GR1[Transmission Grid]
    T1 -->|Tailrace| DR1[Downstream River]
    IN1[Intake with Trash Racks] --> R1
    SP1[Spillway Gates] --> DR1
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Nuclear Power Plant (PWR Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    FC1[Fuel Assemblies U-235] --> RE1[Reactor Core]
    CR1[Control Rods B4C] --> RE1
    RE1 -->|Primary Loop H2O| SG1[Steam Generator]
    SG1 -->|Secondary Steam| TU1[Turbine Set]
    TU1 --> GE1[Generator]
    GE1 --> TX1[Transformer]
    TX1 --> GD1[Grid]
    TU1 --> CN1[Condenser]
    CN1 -->|Feedwater| SG1
    RE1 -->|Hot Leg| PUM1[Reactor Coolant Pump]
    PUM1 -->|Cold Leg| RE1
    SH1[Shielding Concrete] --> RE1
    CT1[Containment Dome] --> RE1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide:** Two loops are visible. The *primary loop* (RE1 ↔ SG1) is highly radioactive and isolated. The *secondary loop* (SG1 ↔ TU1 ↔ CN1) is non-radioactive and identical to a conventional thermal plant. This double-loop design is what makes Pressurized Water Reactors (PWR) the safest commercial design.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**List any six major components of a modern steam power plant.**

**Model Answer:**

A modern thermal (steam) power plant consists of the following major components:

1. **Coal Handling Plant (CHP)** — receives and prepares coal for combustion.
2. **Pulverizer / Mill** — grinds coal into fine powder for efficient combustion.
3. **Boiler (Steam Generator)** — converts water into superheated steam.
4. **Economiser** — preheats feedwater using flue gas heat.
5. **Superheater** — raises steam temperature above saturation.
6. **Steam Turbine (HP–IP–LP)** — extracts work from steam expansion.
7. **Condenser** — condenses exhaust steam back to liquid.
8. **Alternator / Generator** — converts mechanical work to electricity.
9. **Cooling Tower** — rejects condenser heat to atmosphere.
10. **Transformers & Switchyard** — steps up voltage for grid transmission.

*(Any six well-explained components fetch full 3 marks.)*

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**State the significance of the Moderator and Control Rods in a nuclear reactor.**

**Model Answer:**

- **Moderator:** A material (e.g., light water, heavy water, or graphite) placed in the reactor core to **slow down the fast neutrons released during fission** to thermal energies (~ 0.025 eV). Slow neutrons have a much higher probability of causing further fission in U-235, sustaining the chain reaction.

- **Control Rods:** Made of strong neutron absorbers such as **Boron Carbide (B₄C)** or **Cadmium (Cd)**. They are inserted into or withdrawn from the core to **regulate the rate of fission**. By absorbing excess neutrons, they maintain the **multiplication factor k = 1** (critical condition) for steady power output.

---

---

## 📘 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**(a)** With the help of a neat line diagram, explain the working of a **coal-fired thermal power plant** and describe the function of any **four major components**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A thermal power plant has an installed capacity of **200 MW**. During a year it generates **1200 million units** (i.e., $1.2 \times 10^9$ kWh). Compute:
- (i) **Plant Load Factor (PLF)**
- (ii) **Capacity Factor (CF)** if the plant ran for 7000 hours.

**(7 Marks)**

#### 🔹 Model Solution — Part (a)

A coal-fired thermal power plant converts the chemical energy of coal into electrical energy through the **Rankine Cycle**. The working fluid (water) undergoes a closed loop of heating, vaporization, expansion, condensation, and pumping.

**Schematic Flow:**

$$\text{Coal} \xrightarrow{\text{combustion}} \text{Heat} \rightarrow \text{Water} \xrightarrow{\text{boiler}} \text{Steam} \xrightarrow{\text{turbine}} \text{Work} \rightarrow \text{Generator} \rightarrow \text{Electricity}$$

**Functions of Four Major Components:**

1. **Boiler:** Burns pulverized coal in a furnace to transfer heat to water tubes, producing **superheated steam** (typically at 150–250 bar, 540–600 °C).
2. **Steam Turbine:** The high-energy steam is expanded through HP, IP, and LP stages. The pressure drop converts enthalpy into **rotational mechanical work**.
3. **Condenser:** Exhaust steam from the LP turbine is condensed using cooling water at low pressure (≈ 0.05 bar). This creates a **vacuum** that boosts turbine efficiency.
4. **Alternator:** Coupled to the turbine shaft, it converts mechanical rotation (3000 rpm for 50 Hz) into **3-phase AC electricity** via Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction.

#### 🔹 Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given Data:**

- Installed Capacity $P_{\text{inst}} = 200 \text{ MW} = 200{,}000 \text{ kW}$
- Energy Generated $E_{\text{gen}} = 1.2 \times 10^9 \text{ kWh}$
- Hours in year $= 365 \times 24 = 8760 \text{ h}$
- Operating Hours $= 7000 \text{ h}$

**(i) Plant Load Factor:**

$$\text{PLF} = \dfrac{E_{\text{gen}}}{P_{\text{inst}} \times \text{Hours in year}} \times 100$$

$$\text{PLF} = \dfrac{1.2 \times 10^9}{200{,}000 \times 8760} \times 100$$

$$\text{PLF} = \dfrac{1.2 \times 10^9}{1.752 \times 10^9} \times 100 = 0.6849 \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\text{PLF} \approx 68.49\%}$$

**(ii) Capacity Factor:**

$$\text{CF} = \dfrac{E_{\text{gen}}}{P_{\text{inst}} \times \text{Operating hours}} \times 100$$

$$\text{CF} = \dfrac{1.2 \times 10^9}{200{,}000 \times 7000} \times 100$$

$$\text{CF} = \dfrac{1.2 \times 10^9}{1.4 \times 10^9} \times 100 = 0.8571 \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\text{CF} \approx 85.71\%}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** A high Capacity Factor (85.71 %) indicates that when the plant was running, it operated near full load. However, the lower PLF (68.49 %) shows the plant was offline for ~ 1760 hours, likely due to maintenance or grid outages.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**(a)** Explain the **classification of hydro power plants** based on head and available flow, with suitable examples of turbines used. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A hydroelectric station has a **net head of 80 m**, **discharge of 30 m³/s**, and **overall efficiency of 86 %**. Calculate the **electrical power generated** and the **annual energy output** if the plant operates at **55 % capacity factor**. **(7 Marks)**

#### 🔹 Model Solution — Part (a)

Hydroelectric plants are classified based on **available head (H)** and **discharge (Q)**:

| Class | Head Range | Discharge | Typical Turbine | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **High Head** | > 300 m | Low (1–10 m³/s) | **Pelton Wheel** (Impulse) | Mountainous terrain, Himachal Pradesh |
| **Medium Head** | 30 – 300 m | Moderate (10 – 100 m³/s) | **Francis Turbine** (Reaction) | Most common type, Kerala projects |
| **Low Head** | < 30 m | Very High (> 100 m³/s) | **Kaplan / Propeller** (Reaction) | Run-of-river, tidal projects |

**Additional Classification Based on Storage:**

- **Storage / Impoundment Plants** — large dam and reservoir; provides water on demand (e.g., Bhakra Nangal).
- **Run-of-River Plants** — minimal storage; power depends on seasonal flow (e.g., small hydel in Kerala).
- **Pumped Storage Plants** — two reservoirs; water pumped uphill during off-peak, released during peak (e.g., Kadamparai, Tamil Nadu).

**Turbine Selection Principle:**

- **Pelton wheel** is chosen for *high head, low flow* because impulse turbines efficiently harness a single jet at high velocity.
- **Francis turbine** is the *workhorse of medium-head* projects; water flows radially inward under pressure.
- **Kaplan turbine** is best for *low head, high flow*; its adjustable blades mimic a ship's propeller for high specific speed.

#### 🔹 Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given Data:**

- Net Head $H = 80 \text{ m}$
- Discharge $Q = 30 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$
- Overall efficiency $\eta = 0.86$
- Density $\rho = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- Capacity Factor $\text{CF} = 0.55$
- Hours per year $= 8760 \text{ h}$

**(i) Electrical Power Generated:**

$$P_{\text{electrical}} = \eta \, \rho \, g \, Q \, H$$

$$P = 0.86 \times 1000 \times 9.81 \times 30 \times 80$$

**Step-by-step multiplication:**

$$1000 \times 9.81 = 9810$$

$$9810 \times 30 = 294{,}300$$

$$294{,}300 \times 80 = 23{,}544{,}000$$

$$23{,}544{,}000 \times 0.86 = 20{,}247{,}840 \text{ W}$$

$$\boxed{P_{\text{electrical}} \approx 20.25 \text{ MW}}$$

**(ii) Annual Energy Output:**

$$E_{\text{annual}} = P_{\text{inst}} \times 8760 \times \text{CF}$$

$$E_{\text{annual}} = 20.25 \times 10^6 \times 8760 \times 0.55$$

$$E_{\text{annual}} = 20.25 \times 10^6 \times 4818$$

$$E_{\text{annual}} = 97{,}564{,}500{,}000 \text{ Wh} \approx 9.756 \times 10^{10} \text{ Wh}$$

$$\boxed{E_{\text{annual}} \approx 97.56 \text{ GWh (or 97,564 MWh)}}$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** The capacity factor of 55 % is typical for run-of-river hydro plants in India, where seasonal flow variations reduce utilization. A storage hydro plant could achieve 80 – 90 % CF.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Unit Confusion (Most Frequent):** In $P = \eta \rho g Q H$, students use $g = 9.8$ or $10$ inconsistently. KTU examiners award full marks only for $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ unless stated otherwise.
> 2. **Forgetting to Convert MW to kW** when computing PLF or CF — both numerator and denominator must be in the same unit.
> 3. **Confusing PLF and CF** — PLF uses *calendar hours*; CF uses *actual operating hours*. Mixing them loses 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Missing the "Overall Efficiency" Multiplication** — students often write $P = \rho g Q H$ without multiplying by $\eta_t \times \eta_g$, costing 2 marks.
> 5. **Inadequate Diagrams in Thermal Plant Question** — a labelled schematic is mandatory in 7-mark questions; an arrow flow diagram without labels fetches only 2 of 7 marks.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU 2024 Revision Punchlist — Conventional Energy Conversion Plants**

- ✅ **Thermal plants** work on the **Rankine cycle**; key components are **Boiler → Turbine → Condenser → Pump**.
- ✅ **Rankine Efficiency:** $\eta = \dfrac{(h_3 - h_4) - (h_2 - h_1)}{h_3 - h_2}$
- ✅ **Hydro power** equation: $\boxed{P = \eta \rho g Q H}$ — remember SI units throughout.
- ✅ **Hydro turbine selection:** Pelton (high head), Francis (medium head), Kaplan (low head).
- ✅ **Nuclear fission** of U-235 releases **~ 200 MeV** per event; **~ 190 MeV recoverable**.
- ✅ **Mass–Energy:** $E = \Delta m c^2$; $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s.
- ✅ **PLF** uses *calendar hours*; **CF** uses *operating hours* — never interchange.
- ✅ **Plant efficiency** increases with **higher boiler pressure** and **higher superheat temperature** (subject to material limits).
- ✅ **Hydro** has the **highest efficiency (~ 90 %)** and **longest life (50–100 yrs)**.
- ✅ **Nuclear** has the **lowest carbon footprint** (~ 12 g CO₂/kWh) among conventional sources.
- ✅ **Key Indian examples:** NTPC Vindhyachal (Thermal), Tehri Dam (Hydro), Kudankulam (Nuclear).
- ✅ Always convert: **1 kWh = 3.6 × 10⁶ J**, **1 MW = 1000 kW**.
- ✅ In nuclear reactors, **moderator slows neutrons**; **control rods absorb neutrons** — do not mix their roles.
- ✅ **Density of water = 1000 kg/m³** and **g = 9.81 m/s²** are non-negotiable constants for all hydro numericals.
- ✅ In a **PWR**, there are **two separate loops** — primary (radioactive) and secondary (non-radioactive).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Combined Cycle Power Plants
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT614 - ENERGY ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Combined Cycle Power Plants

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 1 — COMBINED CYCLE POWER PLANTS

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Combined Cycle Power Plant – CCPP):**
> A Combined Cycle Power Plant is an integrated power generation system that sequentially combines two (or more) distinct thermodynamic cycles — typically the **Brayton (Gas Turbine) cycle** as the *topping cycle* and the **Rankine (Steam Turbine) cycle** as the *bottoming cycle* — such that the otherwise rejected waste heat from the high-temperature cycle is recovered and reused as the energy input for the low-temperature cycle, yielding a system thermal efficiency significantly greater than that achievable by either cycle operating independently.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Relay Race of Energy"

Imagine a relay race where the first runner (Gas Turbine) is fast but extremely wasteful — he runs 100 m but is so exhausted that he throws away **60–70 %** of his remaining energy as hot, screaming breath into the air. In a conventional power plant, that breath (exhaust heat) goes straight to the atmosphere — pure waste.

In a **Combined Cycle**, we station a clever second runner (Steam Turbine / Heat Recovery Steam Generator) directly behind him. The first runner's hot breath boils water in a kettle (HRSG), the steam pushes the second runner's pistons (Steam Turbine), and BOTH runners deliver medals (kilowatts) to the same finish line (electrical grid).

**Result:** Energy that would have been **wasted** becomes **useful work**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — Why Combined Cycles?**
> Modern combined cycle plants achieve **thermal efficiencies of 55–62 %** (LHV basis), compared to ~35–38 % for conventional sub-critical steam plants and ~32–40 % for open-cycle gas turbines. This makes CCPPs the **benchmark technology** for new fossil-fuel-based generating stations worldwide.

### Key Terminology (KTU Board Standard)

| Term | Bold Definition |
|---|---|
| **Topping Cycle** | The high-temperature cycle operating first (Brayton / Gas Turbine). |
| **Bottoming Cycle** | The low-temperature cycle receiving waste heat (Rankine / Steam Turbine). |
| **HRSG** | **Heat Recovery Steam Generator** — the boiler without an active combustor that captures GT exhaust heat. |
| **Stack Temperature** | The temperature of exhaust gases leaving the HRSG chimney, typically **400–450 K**. |
| **Pinch Point** | The minimum temperature difference between the hot gas and the working fluid inside the HRSG — a **critical design constraint** typically **10–20 K**. |
| **Supplementary Firing (Duct Firing)** | Adding fuel into the HRSG to raise steam temperature beyond gas-turbine exhaust temperature. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics to Remember:**
> - Specific heat of air, **cp,air ≈ 1.005 kJ/(kg·K)**
> - Specific heat of combustion gas, **cp,gas ≈ 1.147 kJ/(kg·K)**
> - Air–Fuel ratio for natural gas, **A/F ≈ 50–70** (stoichiometric ≈ 17.2)
> - Typical HRSG exit gas temperature, **T_stack ≈ 400–450 K**
> - LHV of natural gas, **CV ≈ 50,000 kJ/kg**

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architectural Configuration of a CCPP

The plant is conceptually two power cycles in series:

1. **Gas Turbine (Brayton) Loop** — air is compressed, fuel is burned, hot gas expands through the turbine producing shaft work, then exhaust leaves at high temperature (typically **800–900 K**).
2. **Steam Turbine (Rankine) Loop** — the hot exhaust passes through an **HRSG** where feedwater is converted to superheated steam, which expands through a steam turbine producing additional shaft work.

Both turbines are coupled to **separate electrical generators** (single-shaft arrangements also exist), and their outputs are summed at the grid bus.

### 2.2 Why This Combination Works — The Thermodynamic Logic

- The Brayton cycle has high **peak temperature** (T₃ ≈ 1300–1500 K) → high **Carnot potential** at the top end.
- The Rankine cycle efficiently handles **latent heat transfer** at moderate temperatures (T ≈ 500–800 K), which is precisely the temperature range of the GT exhaust.
- By cascading heat from a high-$\Delta$T cycle to a low-$\Delta$T cycle, the **area under the T–s diagram** (representing useful work) is dramatically enlarged while the heat rejected to the environment (Q_out) is minimized.

### 2.3 The HRSG — Heart of the Combined Cycle

The HRSG is essentially a convective boiler with three thermodynamic zones:

- **Economizer** — preheats feedwater (liquid) using the coolest exhaust gas.
- **Evaporator** — converts water to saturated steam (isothermal at constant pressure).
- **Superheater** — raises steam temperature above saturation (often to **773 K** at 100 bar).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Supplementary (Duct) Firing:** By injecting additional fuel into the HRSG duct, steam temperature can be raised above the GT exhaust temperature, increasing steam turbine output at the cost of slightly lower overall efficiency. Typical applications: peaking power, incremental capacity.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Formula | Variables & Units | Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Gas Turbine Net Work | $W_{net,GT} = \dot{m}_a \, c_{p,a} (T_2 - T_1) \, \frac{\eta_T}{\eta_C} \cdot \text{...}$ | kW | $T_1$ = compressor inlet, $T_2$ = turbine exhaust |
| 2 | Compressor Work (Actual) | $W_C = \dot{m}_a \, c_{p,a} (T_2 - T_1)$ | kW | $\eta_C$ folds into $T_2 - T_1$ |
| 3 | Turbine Work (Actual) | $W_T = \dot{m}_g \, c_{p,g} (T_3 - T_4)$ | kW | $T_3$ = TIT, $T_4$ = exhaust |
| 4 | GT Thermal Efficiency | $\eta_{GT} = \dfrac{W_{net,GT}}{Q_{in,GT}}$ | dimensionless | $Q_{in} = \dot{m}_f \times CV$ |
| 5 | Exhaust Heat Recovered | $Q_{exh} = \dot{m}_g \, c_{p,g} (T_4 - T_{stack})$ | kW | $T_{stack}$ = HRSG exit gas temp |
| 6 | Steam Mass Flow Rate | $\dot{m}_s = \dfrac{Q_{exh}}{h_g - h_f}$ | kg/s | Saturated steam generation |
| 7 | Steam Turbine Work | $W_{ST} = \dot{m}_s \, (h_{in} - h_{out}) \, \eta_{ST}$ | kW | Enthalpy drop × efficiency |
| 8 | Combined Cycle Net Power | $W_{CC} = W_{net,GT} \cdot \eta_{G,GT} + W_{ST} \cdot \eta_{G,ST}$ | kW | Generator efficiencies included |
| 9 | Combined Cycle Efficiency | $\eta_{CC} = \dfrac{W_{CC}}{Q_{in,GT}}$ | dimensionless | Most important single metric |
| 10 | Heat-to-Power Ratio | $R_{H/P} = \dfrac{Q_{exh}}{W_{net,GT}}$ | dimensionless | Used to size HRSG |
| 11 | Pinch Point Relation | $\Delta T_{pinch} = T_{gas,zone} - T_{sat,steam}$ | K | Design constraint, **≥ 10 K** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Combined cycle technology dominates modern utility-scale power generation. The **GE 9HA**, **Siemens SGT-9000HL**, and **MHI J-Series** gas turbines routinely achieve 64 %+ net LHV efficiencies in CCPP configurations, forming the technological backbone of the **global LNG-to-power** value chain. In India, NTPC and Reliance Power operate several GW-scale CCPPs.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Combined Cycle Thermal Efficiency

Let the gas-turbine subsystem have:

- Compressor inlet temperature $T_1$, compressor outlet temperature $T_2$
- Turbine inlet temperature $T_3$ (TIT), turbine exhaust temperature $T_4$
- Air mass flow rate $\dot{m}_a$, fuel mass flow rate $\dot{m}_f$, gas mass flow rate $\dot{m}_g \approx \dot{m}_a + \dot{m}_f$
- Compressor efficiency $\eta_C$, turbine efficiency $\eta_T$, generator efficiency $\eta_{G,GT}$

**Step 1 — Compressor Work:**

$$
W_C = \dot{m}_a \, c_{p,a} (T_2 - T_1)
$$

**Step 2 — Compressor outlet temperature (accounting for isentropic efficiency):**

$$
T_{2s} = T_1 \left( r_p \right)^{\frac{\gamma_a - 1}{\gamma_a}}
$$

$$
T_2 = T_1 + \frac{T_{2s} - T_1}{\eta_C}
$$

*Logic:* The isentropic temperature is the theoretical minimum; real compressors require more work, hence a higher actual outlet temperature. The ratio $(T_{2s} - T_1)/\eta_C$ is the **actual temperature rise** needed.

**Step 3 — Turbine Work:**

$$
W_T = \dot{m}_g \, c_{p,g} (T_3 - T_4)
$$

**Step 4 — Turbine outlet temperature:**

$$
T_{4s} = T_3 \left( \frac{1}{r_p} \right)^{\frac{\gamma_g - 1}{\gamma_g}}
$$

$$
T_4 = T_3 - \eta_T \, (T_3 - T_{4s})
$$

*Logic:* Real turbines extract less work than isentropic; therefore exhaust temperature is **higher** than $T_{4s}$ — more heat is left for the bottoming cycle (a hidden bonus).

**Step 5 — Net Gas Turbine Work:**

$$
W_{net,GT} = W_T - W_C
$$

**Step 6 — Heat Input to Combustion:**

$$
Q_{in} = \dot{m}_f \times CV \quad \text{(using LHV for gas turbines)}
$$

**Step 7 — Heat Available in Exhaust:**

$$
Q_{exh} = \dot{m}_g \, c_{p,g} (T_4 - T_{stack})
$$

*Logic:* Only the portion of exhaust heat **above the HRSG exit temperature** is recoverable; below $T_{stack}$ the gases simply rise up the chimney.

**Step 8 — Steam Generation Rate (saturated steam basis):**

$$
\dot{m}_s = \frac{Q_{exh}}{h_g - h_f}
$$

*Logic:* Energy balance across the HRSG evaporator — heat released by gas equals enthalpy of vaporization of feedwater.

**Step 9 — Steam Turbine Work Output:**

$$
W_{ST} = \dot{m}_s \left[ (h_{in,ST} - h_{out,ST,ideal}) \cdot \eta_{ST} \right] \cdot \eta_{G,ST}
$$

**Step 10 — Combined Cycle Net Power:**

$$
W_{CC} = W_{net,GT} \cdot \eta_{G,GT} + W_{ST}
$$

**Step 11 — Combined Cycle Thermal Efficiency (the master equation):**

$$
\eta_{CC} = \frac{W_{CC}}{Q_{in}} = \frac{W_{net,GT} \cdot \eta_{G,GT} + W_{ST}}{\dot{m}_f \times CV}
$$

*Logic:* The denominator is the **only** external fuel input; both gas and steam turbine outputs are produced from the same fuel stream, hence this single expression captures the true plant efficiency.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Given Data:**
- Compressor inlet: $T_1 = 300$ K, $P_1 = 1$ bar
- Pressure ratio: $r_p = 8$
- Turbine inlet temperature: $T_3 = 1300$ K
- Compressor efficiency: $\eta_C = 0.85$
- Turbine efficiency: $\eta_T = 0.88$
- Generator efficiency (GT): $\eta_{G,GT} = 0.96$
- Air mass flow rate: $\dot{m}_a = 60$ kg/s
- Air–fuel ratio: $A/F = 60$
- Calorific value of fuel: $CV = 43{,}000$ kJ/kg
- For air: $\gamma_a = 1.4$, $c_{p,a} = 1.005$ kJ/(kg·K)
- For gas: $\gamma_g = 1.33$, $c_{p,g} = 1.147$ kJ/(kg·K)
- HRSG exit temperature: $T_{stack} = 450$ K
- Steam conditions: saturated steam at $P_{steam} = 40$ bar; condenser at 0.1 bar
- Generator efficiency (ST): $\eta_{G,ST} = 0.95$
- Steam turbine isentropic efficiency: $\eta_{ST} = 0.85$

**Find:** (a) Net gas turbine power and GT efficiency. (b) Steam generation rate and steam turbine power. (c) Combined cycle efficiency.

#### Part (a) — Gas Turbine Analysis

**Step 1: Isentropic compressor outlet temperature**

$$
T_{2s} = T_1 \cdot r_p^{(\gamma_a - 1)/\gamma_a} = 300 \times 8^{(0.4/1.4)}
$$

$$
8^{0.2857} = 1.8114
$$

$$
T_{2s} = 300 \times 1.8114 = 543.42 \text{ K}
$$

**Step 2: Actual compressor outlet temperature**

$$
T_2 = T_1 + \frac{T_{2s} - T_1}{\eta_C} = 300 + \frac{543.42 - 300}{0.85} = 300 + \frac{243.42}{0.85}
$$

$$
T_2 = 300 + 286.38 = 586.38 \text{ K}
$$

**Step 3: Isentropic turbine outlet temperature**

$$
T_{4s} = \frac{T_3}{r_p^{(\gamma_g - 1)/\gamma_g}} = \frac{1300}{8^{(0.33/1.33)}} = \frac{1300}{1.8114} = 717.65 \text{ K}
$$

**Step 4: Actual turbine outlet temperature**

$$
T_4 = T_3 - \eta_T (T_3 - T_{4s}) = 1300 - 0.88 \times (1300 - 717.65)
$$

$$
T_4 = 1300 - 0.88 \times 582.35 = 1300 - 512.47 = 787.53 \text{ K}
$$

**Step 5: Compressor work**

$$
W_C = \dot{m}_a \cdot c_{p,a} \cdot (T_2 - T_1) = 60 \times 1.005 \times (586.38 - 300)
$$

$$
W_C = 60 \times 1.005 \times 286.38 = 17{,}274.7 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 6: Turbine work** ($\dot{m}_g = \dot{m}_a \times (1 + 1/(A/F)) = 60 \times (61/60) = 61$ kg/s)

$$
W_T = \dot{m}_g \cdot c_{p,g} \cdot (T_3 - T_4) = 61 \times 1.147 \times (1300 - 787.53)
$$

$$
W_T = 61 \times 1.147 \times 512.47 = 35{,}852.9 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 7: Net GT power (shaft)**

$$
W_{net,GT} = W_T - W_C = 35{,}852.9 - 17{,}274.7 = 18{,}578.2 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 8: Generator electrical output (GT side)**

$$
W_{el,GT} = W_{net,GT} \times \eta_{G,GT} = 18{,}578.2 \times 0.96 = 17{,}835.1 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 9: Fuel mass flow rate**

$$
\dot{m}_f = \frac{\dot{m}_a}{A/F} = \frac{60}{60} = 1.0 \text{ kg/s}
$$

**Step 10: Heat input**

$$
Q_{in} = \dot{m}_f \times CV = 1.0 \times 43{,}000 = 43{,}000 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 11: GT-only thermal efficiency**

$$
\eta_{GT} = \frac{W_{net,GT}}{Q_{in}} = \frac{18{,}578.2}{43{,}000} = 0.4320 = 43.20 \%
$$

#### Part (b) — HRSG & Steam Turbine Analysis

**Step 12: Heat recoverable from GT exhaust**

$$
Q_{exh} = \dot{m}_g \cdot c_{p,g} \cdot (T_4 - T_{stack}) = 61 \times 1.147 \times (787.53 - 450)
$$

$$
Q_{exh} = 61 \times 1.147 \times 337.53 = 23{,}617.3 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 13: Steam properties at 40 bar (saturated)**
- $h_f = 1087.5$ kJ/kg
- $h_g = 2801.4$ kJ/kg
- $s_g = 6.070$ kJ/(kg·K)

**Step 14: Steam mass flow rate**

$$
\dot{m}_s = \frac{Q_{exh}}{h_g - h_f} = \frac{23{,}617.3}{2801.4 - 1087.5} = \frac{23{,}617.3}{1713.9} = 13.78 \text{ kg/s}
$$

**Step 15: Steam properties at condenser (0.1 bar)**
- $h_f = 191.8$ kJ/kg, $h_{fg} = 2392.8$ kJ/kg
- $s_f = 0.649$ kJ/(kg·K), $s_{fg} = 7.501$ kJ/(kg·K)

**Step 16: Isentropic exit quality at 0.1 bar**

$$
x_{2s} = \frac{s_g - s_f}{s_{fg}} = \frac{6.070 - 0.649}{7.501} = \frac{5.421}{7.501} = 0.7227
$$

**Step 17: Ideal exit enthalpy**

$$
h_{2s} = h_f + x_{2s} \cdot h_{fg} = 191.8 + 0.7227 \times 2392.8 = 191.8 + 1729.3 = 1921.1 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 18: Ideal enthalpy drop**

$$
\Delta h_{ideal} = h_g - h_{2s} = 2801.4 - 1921.1 = 880.3 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 19: Actual enthalpy drop (with $\eta_{ST} = 0.85$)**

$$
\Delta h_{actual} = \eta_{ST} \times \Delta h_{ideal} = 0.85 \times 880.3 = 748.3 \text{ kJ/kg}
$$

**Step 20: Steam turbine shaft power**

$$
W_{ST} = \dot{m}_s \times \Delta h_{actual} = 13.78 \times 748.3 = 10{,}311.6 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 21: ST electrical output**

$$
W_{el,ST} = W_{ST} \times \eta_{G,ST} = 10{,}311.6 \times 0.95 = 9796.0 \text{ kW}
$$

#### Part (c) — Combined Cycle Efficiency

**Step 22: Total electrical output**

$$
W_{CC} = W_{el,GT} + W_{el,ST} = 17{,}835.1 + 9796.0 = 27{,}631.1 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 23: Combined cycle thermal efficiency**

$$
\eta_{CC} = \frac{W_{CC}}{Q_{in}} = \frac{27{,}631.1}{43{,}000} = 0.6426 = \boxed{64.26 \%}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observation:** The combined cycle efficiency (64.26 %) is dramatically higher than the GT-only efficiency (43.20 %) — an absolute gain of **+21 percentage points**. This is achieved with **zero additional fuel input** — purely by **recycling waste heat**. This single numerical result is the most powerful illustration of the value of combined cycle technology and is a frequent 14-mark KTU question.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Full CCPP Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    AIR["Atmospheric Air Intake"] --> COMP["Axial Flow Compressor"]
    FUEL["Natural Gas Fuel Supply"] --> CC["Combustion Chamber"]
    COMP --> CC
    CC --> GTT["Gas Turbine Expansion Section"]
    GTT --> GEN1["GT Synchronous Generator"]
    GTT --> HRSG1["HRSG Superheater Section"]
    HRSG1 --> HRSG2["HRSG Evaporator Section"]
    HRSG2 --> HRSG3["HRSG Economizer Section"]
    HRSG3 --> STK["Stack To Atmosphere"]
    FW["Feedwater From Condenser Pump"] --> HRSG3
    HRSG2 --> STT["Steam Turbine HP IP LP Stages"]
    STT --> GEN2["ST Synchronous Generator"]
    STT --> COND["Surface Condenser"]
    COND --> CPUMP["Condensate Extraction Pump"]
    CPUMP --> FW
    GEN1 --> BUS["Main Electrical Bus Bar"]
    GEN2 --> BUS
    BUS --> GRID["HV Transformer And Grid Export"]
```

### 4.2 Heat & Work Flow Diagram — Energy Cascade Concept

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TOPPING["TOPPING CYCLE BRAYTON HIGH T"]
        A1["Q_in Fuel Energy"] --> A2["Gas Turbine Work W_GT"]
        A2 --> A3["Hot Exhaust Q_exh 800 to 900 K"]
    end
    subgraph BOTTOMING["BOTTOMING CYCLE RANKINE MEDIUM T"]
        B1["Q_exh Recovered In HRSG"] --> B2["Steam Generation h_g minus h_f"]
        B2 --> B3["Steam Turbine Work W_ST"]
    end
    subgraph LOSSES["REJECTED LOSSES"]
        C1["Q_stack 400 to 450 K"] --> C2["Q_condenser 300 to 320 K"]
    end
    A3 --> B1
    A2 --> OUT1["Electrical Output W_GT"]
    B3 --> OUT2["Electrical Output W_ST"]
    C1 --> ENV["Atmosphere"]
    C2 --> ENV
```

### 4.3 Component-Level T-s Diagram (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TSDIAG["T-s SCHEMATIC NOT TO SCALE"]
        P1["1 Compressor Inlet 300 K"] --> P2["2 Compressor Outlet 586 K"]
        P2 --> P3["3 Turbine Inlet 1300 K"]
        P3 --> P4["4 Turbine Exhaust 787 K"]
        P4 --> P5["5 HRSG Stack 450 K"]
        P6["6 Steam Saturation Line"]
        P7["7 Steam Turbine Expansion To Condenser"]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Aid for the Diagrams:**
> - In the **functional block diagram**, follow the working fluid: air → compressor → combustor → GT → exhaust → HRSG → ST → condenser → pump → back to HRSG.
> - In the **energy cascade**, the left-side `TOPPING` block rejects heat into the right-side `BOTTOMING` block, which then rejects heat into `LOSSES`.
> - In the T-s diagram, points 1→2→3→4 represent the **Brayton loop**, and points 4→5→6→7 represent the **Rankine bottoming loop**.
