## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Cogeneration System Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Fuel Storage and Supply] --> B[Combustion Chamber or Boiler]
    B --> C[Prime Mover - Gas Turbine or Steam Turbine]
    C --> D[Electrical Generator]
    D --> E[Electrical Output to Grid or Load]
    B --> F[Heat Recovery Steam Generator HRSG]
    F --> G[Process Steam or Hot Water Output]
    C --> H[Waste Heat Stream]
    H --> F
    F --> I[Excess Heat to Condenser or Atmosphere]
    style A fill:#f9c74f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#f8961e,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#43aa8b,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style D fill:#577590,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style F fill:#90be6d,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style G fill:#277da1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Fuel → Combustion → Prime Mover → Generator → Electricity. The same fuel path also drives a **Heat Recovery Steam Generator (HRSG)** that captures exhaust heat for process use. This **dual-output** is the defining feature of a topping-cycle cogen plant.

---

### 4.2 Waste Heat Recovery Block Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Industrial Process - Furnace, Kiln, Engine] --> S1[Hot Waste Stream - Flue Gas or Exhaust]
    S1 --> X1[Heat Exchanger - Economiser, Recuperator, or HRSG]
    X1 --> O1[Useful Output - Hot Water, Steam, or Preheated Air]
    X1 --> X2[Heat Rejection - Stack or Vent at Reduced Temperature]
    O1 --> P2[Process Heating or Power Generation]
    O2[Coolant or Feedwater Inlet] --> X1
    style P1 fill:#f8961e,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style S1 fill:#d62828,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style X1 fill:#06aed5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style O1 fill:#2a9d8f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style P2 fill:#264653,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Hot waste gas from the process enters a heat exchanger, transfers thermal energy to a working fluid (water/air), exits cooler to the stack, and the recovered energy drives downstream process heating or power generation.

---

### 4.3 Topping vs Bottoming Cycle Comparative Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph TC[Topping Cycle Cogeneration]
    TC1[Fuel] --> TC2[High-Grade Electricity First]
    TC2 --> TC3[Waste Heat Recovered]
    TC3 --> TC4[Process Heat Delivered]
    end
    subgraph BC[Bottoming Cycle Cogeneration]
    BC1[Fuel] --> BC2[High-Temperature Process Heat First]
    BC2 --> BC3[Residual Heat to WHR Boiler]
    BC3 --> BC4[Power Generation from Waste Heat]
    end
    style TC1 fill:#f9c74f
    style TC4 fill:#43aa8b,color:#fff
    style BC1 fill:#f9c74f
    style BC4 fill:#577590,color:#fff
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Topping cycles produce power first, then use the heat. Bottoming cycles use heat first, then recover residual for power. The choice depends on the **heat-to-power ratio** of the host industry.

---

### 4.4 Energy Conservation — The 4R Hierarchy Pyramid

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[Step 1 - Reduce - Eliminate Unnecessary Energy Use] --> R2[Step 2 - Reuse - Use Energy for Multiple Tasks e.g. Cogeneration]
    R2 --> R3[Step 3 - Recover - Capture Waste Heat e.g. WHR]
    R3 --> R4[Step 4 - Replace - Switch to Efficient or Renewable Sources]
    style R1 fill:#e63946,color:#fff
    style R2 fill:#f1a208
    style R3 fill:#06aed5,color:#fff
    style R4 fill:#2a9d8f,color:#fff
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The 4R hierarchy is a *prioritization scheme*. Always try *Reduce* first, then *Reuse*, then *Recover*, and finally *Replace*. The most economical energy is the energy you never use.

---

### 4.5 Functional Architecture: Boiler + WHR + Cogeneration (Integrated Plant)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FEED[Energy Input Side]
    FU1[Fuel Coal, Gas, Bagasse, Biogas] --> AR1[Air Preheater via Recuperator]
    AR1 --> CB1[Combustion Chamber]
    end
    subgraph CORE[Conversion and Recovery Core]
    CB1 --> HG1[Hot Gas Stream 800 to 1200 C]
    HG1 --> BO1[Boiler with Water Wall Tubes]

    HG1 --> EC1[Economiser Preheats Feedwater]
    EC1 --> AP1[Air Preheater Outlet to Stack at 150 C]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT[Useful Output Side]
    ST1 --> ST2[Steam Turbine]
    ST2 --> GE1[Generator Electrical Output]
    ST1 --> ST3[Process Steam for Plant Use]
    end
    style FU1 fill:#f9c74f
    style CB1 fill:#f8961e
    style BO1 fill:#d62828,color:#fff
    style EC1 fill:#06aed5,color:#fff
    style ST2 fill:#43aa8b,color:#fff
    style ST3 fill:#577590,color:#fff
```

> **Reading the diagram:** This is a real-world **integrated plant** where (a) fuel combustion, (b) steam generation, (c) economiser-based WHR, and (d) cogeneration output (electrical + process steam) all work together — a true embodiment of energy conservation.

**Define cogeneration. List any four industries where cogeneration is widely adopted.**

**Model Answer:**
Cogeneration (Combined Heat and Power, CHP) is the simultaneous production of **electrical energy** and **useful thermal energy** (heat or steam) from a single fuel source, in an integrated system.

> **Governing efficiency:** $\eta_{cogen} = (W_{e} + Q_{th}) / Q_{fuel}$ — typically 60–80%.

**Four industries:**
1. **Sugar mills** — bagasse-fired cogen plants
2. **Pulp and paper** — black liquor recovery boilers

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between a recuperator and a regenerator as used in waste heat recovery systems.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Recuperator | Regenerator |
|---------|-------------|-------------|

| Storage medium | None (just tubes/plates) | Checker bricks, ceramic balls |

### 📗 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **principles of cogeneration**. Discuss the **topping and bottoming cycles** with neat schematic diagrams. (7 marks)

**(b)** A process plant requires **8 MW** of process steam and **4 MW** of electrical power.
- In **separate generation**: a boiler (η = 82%) supplies steam, and electricity is generated at a plant efficiency of 32%.
- In **cogeneration**: a single system supplies both at an overall cogen efficiency of 78%.

Calculate:
- (i) Fuel input required in each case
- (ii) **Fuel Energy Savings Ratio (FESR)**
- (iii) **Heat-to-Power Ratio (HPR)** of the cogen system

(7 marks)

---

##### **Model Solution (a): Principles of Cogeneration**

**Cogeneration** is the simultaneous generation of **two useful forms of energy** — typically electrical energy (or mechanical) and useful thermal energy (heat/steam) — from a **single fuel source** in a **single integrated system**.

**The fundamental principle** rests on the First Law of Thermodynamics: in conventional power generation, only ~30–40% of fuel energy becomes electricity; the remaining 60–70% is rejected as waste heat. Cogeneration *captures* this rejected heat for productive use, raising overall efficiency to 70–80%.

**Topping Cycle**:
- Fuel → **Power first** (electricity/mechanical) → Waste heat recovered as **process heat** (steam/hot water).
- *Examples:* Gas turbine + HRSG, Steam turbine + extraction/bleed steam
- Best for industries needing more heat than power (HPR > 1)

**Bottoming Cycle**:
- Fuel → **High-temperature process heat first** (e.g., cement kiln, blast furnace) → Residual heat → Waste Heat Boiler → **Power**
- *Examples:* Steel plants, cement kilns, glass furnaces
- Best for industries with very high-temperature processes

**Schematic:**

```
TOPPING:        Fuel → Power Gen → Waste Heat → Process Heat
BOTTOMING:      Fuel → Process Heat → Waste Heat Boiler → Power Gen
```

> **[7 marks — full]**

---

##### **Model Solution (b): Numerical Problem**

**Given:**
- Process steam demand: $Q_{th} = 8$ MW
- Power demand: $W_e = 4$ MW
- Separate: $\eta_{boiler} = 0.82$, $\eta_{grid} = 0.32$
- Cogen: $\eta_{cogen} = 0.78$

**(i) Fuel Input Calculations**

**Separate generation — fuel for steam:**
$$F_{boiler} = \frac{Q_{th}}{\eta_{boiler}} = \frac{8}{0.82} = 9.756 \text{ MW}$$

**Separate generation — fuel for electricity:**
$$F_{grid} = \frac{W_e}{\eta_{grid}} = \frac{4}{0.32} = 12.5 \text{ MW}$$

**Total separate fuel input:**
$$F_{sep} = 9.756 + 12.5 = 22.256 \text{ MW}$$

**Cogeneration fuel input:**
$$F_{cog} = \frac{W_e + Q_{th}}{\eta_{cogen}} = \frac{4 + 8}{0.78} = \frac{12}{0.78} = 15.385 \text{ MW}$$

**(ii) Fuel Energy Savings Ratio (FESR)**
$$\text{FESR} = \frac{F_{sep} - F_{cog}}{F_{sep}} = \frac{22.256 - 15.385}{22.256} = \frac{6.871}{22.256}$$

$$\boxed{\text{FESR} = 0.3088 = 30.88\%}$$

**Interpretation:** Cogeneration saves ~31% of the fuel otherwise required — a major energy and economic win.

**(iii) Heat-to-Power Ratio (HPR)**
$$\text{HPR} = \frac{Q_{th}}{W_e} = \frac{8}{4} = 2.0$$

> **[7 marks — full]**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> 1. **FESR formula trap:** Some students write FESR = $F_{cog}/F_{sep}$. The correct formula is **$(F_{sep} - F_{cog}) / F_{sep}$**. Mixing the numerator–denominator order will cost you **2 marks** instantly.
> 2. **HPR units:** HPR is **dimensionless** (MW/MW). Do not write it with units — it is a pure ratio.
> 3. **Cogen η trap:** When using the cogen formula $\eta_{cogen} = (W_e + Q_{th}) / Q_{fuel}$, ensure you **sum both useful outputs** in the numerator. A common error is to use only $W_e$ in the numerator.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a)** What is **Waste Heat Recovery (WHR)**? List the **various waste heat recovery devices** and explain in detail the working of an **economiser** with a neat sketch. (7 marks)

**(b)** A waste heat stream at **$T_{in} = 450 °C$** enters a heat exchanger and exits at **$T_{out} = 180 °C$**. The ambient temperature is **$T_{amb} = 30 °C$**. Calculate:
- (i) The **WHR efficiency** ($\eta_{WHR}$)
- (ii) The **heat recovered** if the mass flow rate of the waste stream is 5 kg/s with $c_p = 1.05$ kJ/kg·K
- (iii) The **fuel saving per hour** if this recovered heat is used to generate steam at 80% boiler efficiency, displacing furnace oil with NCV = 38,000 kJ/kg

(7 marks)

---

##### **Model Solution (a): WHR and Economiser**

**Definition:**
**Waste Heat Recovery (WHR)** is the process of capturing **residual thermal energy** from industrial exhaust streams (flue gases, hot products, cooling water) and converting it into **useful thermal or electrical energy**.

**Common WHR devices:**

| Device | Function | Typical Application |
|--------|----------|---------------------|
| **Economiser** | Preheats boiler feedwater | All steam boilers |
| **Recuperator** | Preheats combustion air | Furnaces, kilns |
| **Regenerator** | Stores and releases heat cyclically | Glass furnaces, blast furnaces |
| **HRSG** | Generates steam from gas turbine exhaust | Combined cycle plants |
| **Waste Heat Boiler** | Generates steam from process exhaust | Cement, steel, refineries |

**Economiser — Detailed Working:**
An economiser is a **heat exchanger** placed in the **flue gas path** of a boiler, **downstream of the evaporator and superheater** and **upstream of the air preheater**. It transfers heat from the **hot flue gases** to the **incoming boiler feedwater**, raising the water temperature before it enters the boiler drum.

**Working Principle (Schematic):**

```
Flue Gas (800°C from boiler exit)
       ↓ ↓ ↓ ↓
  ╔════════════════════╗
  ║   ECONOMISER       ║  ← Water tubes (inlet 105°C → outlet 200°C)
  ╚════════════════════╝
       ↓ ↓ ↓ ↓
Flue Gas (180°C to stack)
```

**Key benefits:**
- Reduces fuel consumption by 5–10%
- Lowers stack temperature, reducing $L_1$ (dry flue gas loss)
- Preheated water → less thermal stress on boiler tubes

> **[7 marks — full]**

---

##### **Model Solution (b): Numerical Problem**

**Given:**
- $T_{in} = 450 °C = 723$ K
- $T_{out} = 180 °C = 453$ K
- $T_{amb} = 30 °C = 303$ K
- $\dot{m} = 5$ kg/s
- $c_p = 1.05$ kJ/kg·K
- $\eta_{boiler} = 0.80$
- NCV = 38,000 kJ/kg

**(i) WHR Efficiency**

$$\eta_{WHR} = \frac{T_{in} - T_{out}}{T_{in} - T_{amb}} = \frac{450 - 180}{450 - 30} = \frac{270}{420}$$

$$\boxed{\eta_{WHR} = 0.6429 = 64.29\%}$$

**(ii) Heat Recovered**
$$\dot{Q}_{recovered} = \dot{m} \times c_p \times (T_{in} - T_{out})$$
$$\dot{Q}_{recovered} = 5 \times 1.05 \times (450 - 180) = 5 \times 1.05 \times 270$$

$$\dot{Q}_{recovered} = 1417.5 \text{ kW} = 1.4175 \text{ MW}$$

**(iii) Fuel Saving per Hour**

Heat recovered per hour:
$$Q_{hourly} = 1417.5 \times 3600 = 5,103,000 \text{ kJ/h}$$

Effective steam heat delivered (after boiler efficiency, since it is used to generate steam):
$$Q_{steam,delivered} = Q_{hourly} \times \eta_{boiler} = 5,103,000 \times 0.80 = 4,082,400 \text{ kJ/h}$$

Fuel saved:
$$m_{fuel,saved} = \frac{Q_{steam,delivered}}{\text{NCV}} = \frac{4,082,400}{38,000}$$

$$\boxed{m_{fuel,saved} = 107.43 \text{ kg/h}}$$

> **[7 marks — full]**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Part (b):**
> 1. **Temperature units trap:** WHR efficiency works identically in °C and K (because the differences cancel). **But** if the question uses $T_{in}$ in a state equation or gas constant formula, **convert to Kelvin** first.
> 2. **Boiler efficiency position:** In part (iii), some students forget to apply $\eta_{boiler}$ because they assume the recovered heat is "free." The heat still has to **boil water** to become useful steam, so apply $\eta_{boiler}$ before dividing by NCV.
> 3. **Unit consistency:** $\dot{m} \times c_p \times \Delta T$ in SI gives **kW** (not kJ/s × kJ/kg·K is kJ/s = kW). To convert to **kJ/h**, multiply kW by 3600.

---

### 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Energy conservation** is *demand-side management*, not energy *reduction*. It means doing more useful work with less input.
- ✅ The **First Law** guarantees energy is conserved — but **exergy** is destroyed; conservation strategies minimize exergy destruction.
- ✅ **Cogeneration** produces **two useful outputs** from one fuel: electricity + useful heat. Efficiency: **70–80%** vs **30–40%** for separate generation.
- ✅ **Topping cycle** = power first, then heat. **Bottoming cycle** = heat first, then power.
- ✅ **FESR** measures fuel saved by going cogen: $\text{FESR} = (F_{sep} - F_{cog}) / F_{sep}$. Typical range: **20–35%**.
- ✅ **HPR** (Heat-to-Power Ratio) determines which cycle suits an industry. Sugar/paper = high HPR (topping). Steel/glass = low HPR (bottoming).
- ✅ **WHR devices:** Economiser, Recuperator, Regenerator, HRSG, Waste Heat Boiler.
- ✅ **WHR efficiency formula:** $\eta_{WHR} = (T_{in} - T_{out}) / (T_{in} - T_{amb})$ — works in °C or K.
- ✅ **Boiler efficiency** can be measured **directly** (input–output) or **indirectly** (six standard losses $L_1$ to $L_6$).
- ✅ **The 4R Hierarchy:** Reduce → Reuse → Recover → Replace. Always present in this order in KTU answers.
- ✅ **Gas constant temperatures:** Use **Kelvin** for absolute and gas-law calculations; differences are fine in °C.
- ✅ **KTU Board Answer Rule:** Always define every symbol, then write the formula, then substitute numerical values with units, then box the final answer. Marks are awarded for **each step**, not just the final number.
- ✅ **Common 14-mark question patterns:** (i) Cogeneration with FESR calculation, (ii) Boiler efficiency by indirect method with full loss breakdown, (iii) WHR efficiency + fuel-saving calculation, (iv) Comparison of topping vs bottoming cycle.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Waste to Energy Technologies - Gasification, Pyrolysis, Fermentation, Anaerobic Digestion: Compressed Biogas
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECHT614 - ENERGY ENGINEERING AND MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Waste to Energy Technologies - Gasification, Pyrolysis, Fermentation, Anaerobic Digestion: Compressed Biogas

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Waste-to-Energy (WtE) Technologies

**Waste-to-Energy (WtE)** refers to a suite of engineered processes that convert residual biomass, municipal solid waste (MSW), industrial effluents, and agricultural residues into usable forms of energy — heat, electricity, or transportable gaseous/liquid fuels. Within the KTU 2024 OECHT614 framework, the four pillar technologies studied under Module 4 are **Gasification, Pyrolysis, Fermentation, and Anaerobic Digestion (AD)**, with **Compressed Biogas (CBG)** as the downstream product stream of AD.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme):** Students are expected to differentiate *thermochemical* routes (Gasification, Pyrolysis) from *biochemical* routes (Fermentation, Anaerobic Digestion). Examiners frequently test this binary classification.

## 1.2 Formal Definitions Aligned with KTU Terminology

### Gasification
**Gasification** is a *thermochemical conversion* process in which carbonaceous feedstock is partially oxidized at **elevated temperatures (typically 700 °C – 1200 °C)** in an oxygen-deficient environment (sub-stoichiometric air or pure O₂) to produce a combustible synthesis gas (syngas) primarily composed of **CO, H₂, CO₂, CH₄, and N₂**.

> [!NOTE]
> The equivalence ratio (ER) for gasification is strictly maintained between **0.25 – 0.40** to suppress full combustion. This is a frequently asked numerical parameter in KTU exams.

### Pyrolysis
**Pyrolysis** is the *thermal decomposition* of organic matter in the **complete absence of oxygen** (or with negligible oxygen) at temperatures between **400 °C – 900 °C**. The three products are solid char, liquid bio-oil, and non-condensable pyrolytic gases, with distribution dependent on heating rate and residence time.

### Fermentation
**Fermentation** is a *biochemical conversion* process wherein microorganisms (primarily yeasts such as *Saccharomyces cerevisiae*) anaerobically metabolize simple sugars (glucose, fructose, sucrose) into **ethanol and CO₂** through the Embden-Meyerhof-Parnas (EMP) glycolytic pathway.

### Anaerobic Digestion (AD)
**Anaerobic Digestion** is a *biochemical decomposition* process in which a consortium of microorganisms breaks down complex organic substrates (in the absence of oxygen) through four sequential stages — **Hydrolysis, Acidogenesis, Acetogenesis, and Methanogenesis** — yielding a methane-rich **biogas (typically 50–70% CH₄ and 30–40% CO₂)**.

### Compressed Biogas (CBG)
**Compressed Biogas (CBG)** is purified, compressed biogas with methane purity **≥ 90%** (typically **≥ 95%** under India's SATAT scheme) compressed to pressures of **200 – 250 bar**, suitable for use as a transport fuel (CNG substitute) or for grid injection.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world Analogy — The Four Cooking Methods:**
> Imagine cooking a vegetable to extract its essence:
> - **Pyrolysis** = charring the vegetable on a dry tawa (no air) → you get char (solid), smoke (gas), and condensed droplets (bio-oil).
> - **Gasification** = heating the vegetable in limited air (a controlled, smoldering fire) → you get a combustible gas flame (syngas).
> - **Fermentation** = leaving fruit juice in a closed jar → yeast eats sugar, releases ethanol + CO₂ (think wine-making).
> - **Anaerobic Digestion** = sealing wet kitchen waste in an air-tight tank → microbes eat it slowly, releasing methane-rich biogas (think swamp gas or a gobar-gas plant).
> - **Compressed Biogas** = the methane gas from AD, scrubbed clean, squeezed into a steel cylinder at car-fuel pressure (200+ bar) → ready to fuel your car.

This analogy makes the distinction between **thermochemical** (fire-based) and **biochemical** (microbe-based) routes intuitively obvious.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Gasification — Process Architecture

Gasification occurs in four distinct reaction zones inside a gasifier:

1. **Drying Zone (100 – 200 °C):** Moisture evaporation. Endothermic.
2. **Pyrolysis Zone (200 – 600 °C):** Volatiles released; char formed.
3. **Oxidation Zone (700 – 1200 °C):** Exothermic combustion of char and volatiles with limited O₂.
4. **Reduction Zone (600 – 900 °C):** Endothermic gas-solid reactions producing syngas.

### Key Heterogeneous Reactions (Reduction Zone)

The quality of syngas is governed by these critical reactions:

| # | Reaction Name | Chemical Equation | Enthalpy (ΔH at 25 °C) | Nature |
|---|---------------|-------------------|-------------------------|--------|
| 1 | Boudouard | C + CO₂ → 2CO | **+172.5 kJ/mol** | Endothermic |
| 2 | Water-Gas (Primary) | C + H₂O → CO + H₂ | **+131.4 kJ/mol** | Endothermic |
| 3 | Methanation | C + 2H₂ → CH₄ | **−74.8 kJ/mol** | Exothermic |
| 4 | Water-Gas Shift (WGS) | CO + H₂O → CO₂ + H₂ | **−41.2 kJ/mol** | Exothermic |
| 5 | Partial Oxidation | C + ½O₂ → CO | **−111.4 kJ/mol** | Exothermic |

### Gasifying Agents

| Agent | Syngas Composition (Approx.) | Calorific Value (MJ/Nm³) |
|-------|------------------------------|---------------------------|
| Air | 50% N₂, 18% H₂, 22% CO, 10% CO₂ | **4 – 7** |
| Pure O₂ | 32% H₂, 45% CO, 15% CO₂ | **10 – 18** |
| Steam | 50% H₂, 20% CO, 25% CO₂ | **10 – 20** |

## 2.2 Pyrolysis — Modes of Operation

| Mode | Temperature (°C) | Heating Rate | Residence Time | Major Product |
|------|------------------|--------------|----------------|---------------|
| Slow Pyrolysis (Carbonization) | 400 – 600 | Low (5 – 30 °C/min) | Hours – Days | **Char (35%)** |
| Fast Pyrolysis | 500 – 800 | High (>1000 °C/s) | <2 seconds | **Bio-oil (75%)** |
| Flash Pyrolysis | 800 – 1000 | Very High | <0.5 seconds | **Bio-oil/Gas** |
| Vacuum Pyrolysis | 300 – 500 | Moderate | Seconds | **Bio-oil** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** "Fast pyrolysis" is the industrial standard for bio-oil production. Remember the mnemonic **"Hot & Fast = Liquid, Cool & Slow = Solid"**.

## 2.3 Fermentation — Ethanol Pathway

The stoichiometric alcoholic fermentation of glucose follows the **Gay-Lussac Equation**:

$$C_6H_{12}O_6 \xrightarrow{\text{Yeast}} 2\,C_2H_5OH + 2\,CO_2 + \text{Energy (ATP)}$$

**Theoretical Yield of Ethanol:**
- From **1 mole of glucose (180 g)** → **2 moles of ethanol (92 g)** → Mass yield = **51.1 %**
- Industrial yeast strains achieve **90 – 95%** of this theoretical maximum.

## 2.4 Anaerobic Digestion — The Four-Stage Microbiology

| Stage | Microorganism Class | Substrate | Product | Key Enzyme |
|-------|---------------------|-----------|---------|------------|
| **1. Hydrolysis** | Fermentative Bacteria | Polymers (carbs, proteins, lipids) | Monomers (sugars, AAs, FAs) | Hydrolases |
| **2. Acidogenesis** | Acidogenic Bacteria | Monomers | VFAs, alcohols, H₂, CO₂ | — |
| **3. Acetogenesis** | Acetogenic Bacteria | VFAs, alcohols | Acetate, H₂, CO₂ | — |
| **4. Methanogenesis** | Methanogenic Archaea | Acetate, H₂/CO₂ | **CH₄, CO₂** | Methyl-Coenzyme M |

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---------------------|------------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | Equivalence Ratio (ER) | $ER = \dfrac{\text{Actual Air}}{\text{Stoichiometric Air}}$ | dimensionless | Gasifier control |
| 2 | Cold Gas Efficiency (CGE) | $CGE = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{\text{gas}} \times LHV_{\text{gas}}}{\dot{m}_{\text{feed}} \times LHV_{\text{feed}}} \times 100$ | % | Gasifier performance |
| 3 | Carbon Conversion Efficiency (CCE) | $CCE = \dfrac{C_{\text{converted to gas}}}{C_{\text{in feed}}} \times 100$ | % | Gasification |
| 4 | Theoretical Ethanol Yield | $Y_{EtOH} = \dfrac{M_{EtOH}}{M_{Glucose}} = \dfrac{2 \times 46}{180} = 0.5111$ | kg/kg | Fermentation |
| 5 | Biogas Yield (Biochemical Methane Potential) | $B_0$ | Nm³ CH₄/kg VS | AD |
| 6 | Methane Fraction from Acetate | $CH_4 = 0.25 \times COD_{\text{removed}}$ | Nm³/kg COD | AD design |
| 7 | Compressed Biogas Energy | $E_{CBG} = V_{CH_4} \times LHV_{CH_4}$ | MJ | CBG utility |
| 8 | Pressure Work (Compression) | $W_{\text{comp}} = \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} R T_1 \left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1\right]$ | kJ/kg | CBG compression |
| 9 | Calorific Value (Dulong) | $CV = 0.338\,C + 1.428\left(H - \dfrac{O}{8}\right) + 0.095\,S$ | MJ/kg | Solid fuel analysis |
| 10 | Gas Density (Ideal Gas) | $\rho = \dfrac{P \cdot M}{R \cdot T}$ | kg/m³ | CBG storage |

> [!NOTE]
> The **calorific value of pure methane (CH₄)** is **LHV = 50 MJ/kg (35.8 MJ/Nm³)** and **HHV = 55.5 MJ/kg**. These constants appear frequently in numerical problems.

## 2.6 Engineering Utility in Real-World Applications

- **Gasification:** Decentralized rural electrification via biomass-to-power gasifiers (e.g., 100 kW downdraft gasifiers in Kerala).
- **Pyrolysis:** Biochar for soil amendment; bio-oil for boiler fuel substitution.
- **Fermentation:** First-generation (sugarcane molasses) and second-generation (lignocellulosic) ethanol for India's **E20 blending program**.
- **Anaerobic Digestion:** India's **SATAT (Sustainable Alternative Towards Affordable Transportation)** scheme targets 15 MMT of CBG by 2030.
- **CBG:** Drop-in fuel for CNG vehicles; grid injection for domestic piped-gas networks.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Theoretical Ethanol Yield from Glucose

**Problem Statement:** A fermentation vessel contains **1000 kg of glucose**. Assuming complete conversion per the Gay-Lussac equation, calculate the theoretical mass of ethanol and CO₂ produced.

### Step 1: Write the Balanced Stoichiometric Equation

$$C_6H_{12}O_6 \longrightarrow 2\,C_2H_5OH + 2\,CO_2$$

### Step 2: Identify Molecular Masses

- Molar mass of glucose $C_6H_{12}O_6$ = (6 × 12) + (12 × 1) + (6 × 16) = **180 g/mol**
- Molar mass of ethanol $C_2H_5OH$ = (2 × 12) + (6 × 1) + (1 × 16) = **46 g/mol**
- Molar mass of $CO_2$ = **44 g/mol**

### Step 3: Apply Stoichiometric Ratios

From the balanced equation, **1 mole of glucose** yields **2 moles of ethanol** and **2 moles of CO₂**.

$$\text{Moles of glucose} = \dfrac{1000 \text{ kg}}{180 \text{ kg/kmol}} = 5.556 \text{ kmol}$$

### Step 4: Calculate Moles of Ethanol Produced

$$\text{Moles of ethanol} = 2 \times 5.556 = 11.111 \text{ kmol}$$

### Step 5: Convert to Mass

$$\text{Mass of ethanol} = 11.111 \text{ kmol} \times 46 \text{ kg/kmol} = 511.11 \text{ kg}$$

### Step 6: Verify Theoretical Yield Percentage

$$Y_{EtOH} = \dfrac{511.11}{1000} \times 100 = 51.11\%$$

This matches the known **51.1% theoretical yield** of ethanol from glucose — a high-yield KTU problem.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Cold Gas Efficiency of a Downdraft Biomass Gasifier

**Given Data:**
- Biomass feed rate $\dot{m}_{\text{feed}} = 200$ kg/hr
- Feed LHV = **17 MJ/kg** (typical for wood chips with ~10% moisture)
- Syngas flow rate $\dot{m}_{\text{gas}} = 350$ Nm³/hr
- Syngas LHV = **5.5 MJ/Nm³**
- Syngas density at NTP ≈ 1.15 kg/Nm³

### Step 1: Calculate Mass Flow of Syngas

$$\dot{m}_{\text{gas}} = 350 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{hr} \times 1.15 \text{ kg/Nm}^3 = 402.5 \text{ kg/hr}$$

### Step 2: Compute Energy Input from Feed

$$E_{\text{in}} = \dot{m}_{\text{feed}} \times LHV_{\text{feed}} = 200 \times 17 = 3400 \text{ MJ/hr}$$

### Step 3: Compute Energy Output in Syngas

$$E_{\text{out}} = 350 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{hr} \times 5.5 \text{ MJ/Nm}^3 = 1925 \text{ MJ/hr}$$

### Step 4: Apply CGE Formula

$$CGE = \dfrac{E_{\text{out}}}{E_{\text{in}}} \times 100 = \dfrac{1925}{3400} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{CGE = 56.62\%}$$

A typical downdraft gasifier achieves 60 – 75% CGE; our result is in the realistic low-end range, indicating a wet feedstock.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Compressor Work for CBG Production

**Problem:** A biogas stream of $0.5$ Nm³/min at **1.013 bar and 30 °C** must be compressed to **200 bar** for CBG. Calculate the isentropic compressor work using $\gamma = 1.3$ for methane (treat as ideal gas) and $R = 0.518$ kJ/(kg·K).

### Step 1: Convert to Mass Flow

Using methane molar volume (24.47 Nm³/kmol at NTP, but at 30 °C: 24.47 × 303/273 = 27.16 Nm³/kmol):

$$\dot{m} = \dfrac{0.5 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{min}}{27.16 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{kmol}} \times 16 \text{ kg/kmol} = 0.2946 \text{ kg/min}$$

### Step 2: Apply Isentropic Work Equation

For a multi-stage intercooled compressor, the work equation is:

$$W_{\text{comp}} = \dfrac{\gamma}{\gamma - 1} R T_1 \left[\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1\right] \times \dfrac{1}{\eta_{\text{isentropic}}}$$

For **single-stage** (with $\eta_{\text{isentropic}} = 0.75$ typical for CBG compressors):

### Step 3: Substitute Values

$$W_{\text{comp}} = \dfrac{1.3}{0.3} \times 0.518 \times 303.15 \times \left[\left(200\right)^{0.3/1.3} - 1\right] \div 0.75$$

### Step 4: Evaluate the Pressure Ratio Term

$$\left(200\right)^{0.2308} = e^{0.2308 \times \ln 200} = e^{0.2308 \times 5.2983} = e^{1.2229} = 3.396$$

### Step 5: Complete the Calculation

$$W_{\text{comp}} = 4.333 \times 0.518 \times 303.15 \times (3.396 - 1) \div 0.75$$

$$W_{\text{comp}} = 4.333 \times 0.518 \times 303.15 \times 2.396 \div 0.75$$

$$\boxed{W_{\text{comp}} = 2041.3 \text{ kJ/kg of methane}}$$

This value is the **theoretical minimum isentropic work**. In practice, real CBG compressors (which are often multi-stage with intercooling) consume **2200 – 2500 kJ/kg** — a 10-15% overhead due to mechanical and thermal irreversibilities.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Performance of WtE Technologies

| Parameter | Gasification | Pyrolysis | Fermentation | Anaerobic Digestion |
|-----------|--------------|-----------|--------------|---------------------|
| **Temperature (°C)** | 700 – 1200 | 400 – 900 | 25 – 40 (mesophilic) | 35 – 55 (mesophilic/thermophilic) |
| **Oxygen Required** | Limited (ER = 0.25 – 0.40) | None | None | None (strictly anaerobic) |
| **Primary Product** | Syngas (CO + H₂) | Bio-oil / Char / Gas | Ethanol | Biogas (CH₄ + CO₂) |
| **Energy Efficiency** | 60 – 75% (CGE) | 60 – 80% (mass-energy) | 90 – 95% (theoretical) | 30 – 60% (energy recovery) |
| **Residence Time** | Seconds – minutes | Seconds – hours | 24 – 72 hours | 15 – 40 days |
| **Feedstock Flexibility** | High (any biomass) | High | Low (sugars/starch) | High (wet organics) |
| **Catalyst Required** | Optional (Ni-based) | Optional (HZSM-5) | Yeast (biological) | Microbial consortium |
| **Environmental Concern** | Tar, dust | Bio-oil acidity | Stillage disposal | H₂S, NH₃ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Insight:** Notice that **fermentation is the only single-organism process**; all others (especially AD) are **microbial consortia processes** requiring strict environmental control (pH 6.8 – 7.4 for methanogens).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Flow Architecture of WtE Conversion Routes

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Biomass and Organic Waste Feedstock] --> B{Conversion Pathway Selection}
    B -->|Thermochemical Route| C[Gasification]
    B -->|Thermochemical Route| D[Pyrolysis]
    B -->|Biochemical Route| E[Fermentation]
    B -->|Biochemical Route| F[Anaerobic Digestion AD]
    C --> C1[Syngas CO and H2 rich]
    C1 --> C2[Heat and Power Generation]
    C1 --> C3[Fischer Tropsch Liquid Fuels]
    D --> D1{Slow Pyrolysis}
    D --> D2{Fast Pyrolysis}
    D1 --> D1a[Biochar for Soil Amendment]
    D2 --> D2a[Bio oil for Boiler Fuel]
    D2 --> D2b[Pyrolytic Gas Recycle]
    E --> E1[Ethanol Distillation Column]
    E1 --> E2[Anhydrous Ethanol 99.9 percent]
    E2 --> E3[Blending with Petrol E20]
    E2 --> E4[Industrial Solvent Use]
    F --> F1[Raw Biogas CH4 50 to 70 percent]
    F1 --> F2{H2S and Moisture Removal}
    F2 --> F3[CO2 Separation PSA or Water Scrubbing]
    F3 --> F4[Compressed Biogas CBG at 200 bar]
    F4 --> F5[CNG Transport Fuel]
    F4 --> F6[Grid Injection Piped Gas]
    F4 --> F7[Industrial Boiler Fuel]
```

## 4.2 Detailed Block Diagram — Anaerobic Digestion & CBG Purification Train

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Substrate Collection Pit] --> B[Solid Liquid Separator]
    B --> C[Feed Mixing Tank with Recycle]
    C --> D[Hydrolysis Stage]
    D --> E[Acidogenesis Stage]
    E --> F[Acetogenesis Stage]
    F --> G[Methanogenesis Stage]
    G --> H[Raw Biogas Holder]
    H --> I[Desulfurization H2S Scrubber]
    I --> J[Dehumidification Condenser]
    J --> K[CO2 Separation Unit]
    K --> L[Methane Purification to 95 percent]
    L --> M[Compressor Multi Stage 200 bar]
    M --> N[CBG Storage Cascade]
    N --> O[Dispensing Station CNG Vehicles]
    G -.digestate.-> P[Liquid Fertilizer Storage]
    G -.digestate.-> Q[Solid Compost Pit]
```

## 4.3 Gasifier Internal Zone Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Biomass Inlet Top] --> B[Drying Zone 100 to 200 C]
    B --> C[Pyrolysis Zone 200 to 600 C]
    C --> D[Oxidation Zone 700 to 1200 C]
    D --> E[Reduction Zone 600 to 900 C]
    E --> F[Syngas Outlet]
    G[Air or O2 Inlet] --> D
    E -.ash.-> H[Ash Removal Bottom]
```

## 4.4 Pyrolysis Mode Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Biomass Feed] --> B{Target Product}
    B -->|Solid Char| C[Slow Pyrolysis 400 to 600 C]
    B -->|Liquid Bio oil| D[Fast Pyrolysis 500 to 800 C]
    B -->|Gas Fuel| E[Flash Pyrolysis 800 to 1000 C]
    C --> C1[Carbon Black Industry]
    C --> C2[Soil Amendment]
    D --> D1[Bio oil Upgrading]
    D --> D2[Boiler Fuel Direct]
    E --> E1[Internal Combustion Engine]
```

## 4.5 Comparative Process Flow — Fermentation vs. Anaerobic Digestion

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FERMENTATION_PATHWAY
        A1[Glucose or Sucrose Feed] --> A2[Yeast Inoculum]
        A2 --> A3[Fermenter 30 C 48 hours]
        A3 --> A4[Beer Ethanol 8 to 12 percent]
        A4 --> A5[Distillation Column]
        A5 --> A6[Rectified Spirit 95 percent]
        A6 --> A7[ Molecular Sieve Dehydration]
        A7 --> A8[Anhydrous Ethanol 99.9 percent]
    end
    subgraph AD_PATHWAY
        B1[Wet Organic Waste Slurry] --> B2[AD Reactor 35 to 55 C 25 days]
        B2 --> B3[Raw Biogas]
        B2 --> B4[Digestate]
        B3 --> B5[Upgradation Unit]
        B5 --> B6[Compressed Biogas CBG]
    end
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]
**Differentiate between the equivalence ratio (ER) used in gasification and the air-fuel ratio used in combustion. Why is ER maintained below 0.4 in gasifiers?**

**Model Answer:**
- **Combustion** uses an Air-Fuel Ratio (AFR) **greater than or equal to 1** to ensure **complete oxidation** of fuel (e.g., AFR for CH₄ ≈ 17.2).
- **Gasification** uses an **Equivalence Ratio (ER) less than 1** (typically 0.25 – 0.40), defined as the ratio of *actual* air supplied to *stoichiometric* air required.
- **Why ER < 0.4?** A low ER deliberately creates an **oxygen-deficient (reducing) atmosphere** that prevents complete combustion of carbon to CO₂. Instead, partial oxidation produces **CO and H₂** — the combustible components of syngas. If ER exceeds 0.4, the system shifts toward **combustion**, lowering syngas calorific value drastically.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]
**List the four stages of anaerobic digestion and identify the most sensitive stage in terms of process stability.**

**Model Answer:**
The four stages are:
1. **Hydrolysis** — breakdown of polymers to monomers.
2. **Acidogenesis** — conversion of monomers to volatile fatty acids (VFAs).
3. **Acetogenesis** — conversion of VFAs to acetate, H₂, and CO₂.
4. **Methanogenesis** — production of CH₄ from acetate and H₂/CO₂.

The most sensitive stage is **Methanogenesis**, because methanogenic archaea are:
- Strictly **anaerobic** (intolerant to even trace O₂).
- Slow-growing (**doubling time ~ 5–10 days**).
- Highly **pH-sensitive** (optimum pH 6.8 – 7.4; below 6.5, methane production ceases and VFAs accumulate — a condition called "souring").

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – July 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches the working principle of a **downdraft biomass gasifier**. List the various zones and the dominant chemical reactions occurring in each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A downdraft gasifier processes **500 kg/hr** of wood chips with moisture content of **12%** (db basis). The LHV of dry wood is **18 MJ/kg**. The syngas produced has a flow rate of **850 Nm³/hr** with an LHV of **5.8 MJ/Nm³**. Calculate the **Cold Gas Efficiency (CGE)**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

A downdraft gasifier is a **fixed-bed, co-current** gasifier where biomass and air flow in the **same downward direction**. It consists of four distinct zones:

**Zone 1 — Drying Zone (100 – 200 °C):**
- Biomass is dried by the hot updraft of flue gases.
- Reaction: $H_2O_{(l)} \rightarrow H_2O_{(g)}$, Endothermic.

**Zone 2 — Pyrolysis Zone (200 – 600 °C):**
- Biomass devolatilizes, releasing condensable vapors, tars, and non-condensable gases.
- The remaining solid is **char**.
- Reaction: $Biomass \rightarrow Char + Tar + Volatiles$

**Zone 3 — Oxidation (Combustion) Zone (700 – 1200 °C):**
- Char and volatiles react with limited O₂.
- Reaction: $C + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2$ (ΔH = −393.5 kJ/mol)
- Reaction: $2C + O_2 \rightarrow 2CO$ (Partial Oxidation)
- This is the **hottest zone** and supplies heat for endothermic reactions above and below it.

**Zone 4 — Reduction Zone (600 – 900 °C):**
- Hot CO₂ and H₂O are reduced by hot char.
- **Boudouard:** $C + CO_2 \rightarrow 2CO$ (ΔH = +172.5 kJ/mol)
- **Water-Gas:** $C + H_2O \rightarrow CO + H_2$ (ΔH = +131.4 kJ/mol)
- **Water-Gas Shift:** $CO + H_2O \rightarrow CO_2 + H_2$

The syngas exits at the bottom (along with ash). Downdraft gasifiers produce **relatively low-tar syngas** (~ 0.1 – 0.5 g/Nm³), making them ideal for direct engine coupling.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:**
- $\dot{m}_{\text{feed}} = 500$ kg/hr (wet, with 12% moisture on db basis → mc = 12/112 = 10.71% wet basis)
- LHV of dry wood = 18 MJ/kg
- LHV of wet feed = 18 × (1 − 0.1071) = 16.07 MJ/kg
- Syngas flow = 850 Nm³/hr
- Syngas LHV = 5.8 MJ/Nm³

**Step 1: Energy Input**

$$E_{\text{in}} = 500 \text{ kg/hr} \times 16.07 \text{ MJ/kg} = 8035.71 \text{ MJ/hr}$$

**Step 2: Energy Output**

$$E_{\text{out}} = 850 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{hr} \times 5.8 \text{ MJ/Nm}^3 = 4930 \text{ MJ/hr}$$

**Step 3: Cold Gas Efficiency**

$$CGE = \dfrac{4930}{8035.71} \times 100 = \boxed{61.35\%}$$

This CGE is **within the typical industrial range of 60 – 75%** for downdraft wood-chip gasifiers. A higher moisture content in the feed would reduce this value, justifying pre-drying as a pre-treatment step.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** With the help of a process flow diagram, describe the **Compressed Biogas (CBG) production** scheme from agricultural waste. What are the **SATAT scheme** targets? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A biogas plant produces **2000 Nm³/day** of raw biogas with composition: **CH₄ = 60%, CO₂ = 38%, H₂S = 1%, N₂ = 1%**. The gas is upgraded to **95% methane purity** and compressed to **200 bar** for use as automotive fuel. Calculate: **(i)** the volume of pure CH₄ obtained per day, **(ii)** the energy content of CBG (LHV of CH₄ = 35.8 MJ/Nm³), and **(iii)** the equivalent litres of petrol (LHV of petrol = 32 MJ/L). **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**CBG Production Process (from agricultural waste):**

1. **Feedstock Collection & Slurry Preparation:** Agricultural residue (rice straw, sugarcane bagasse, press mud) is mixed with water/cow dung to form a slurry of 8 – 12% Total Solids (TS).
2. **Anaerobic Digester (CSTR / Plug-flow):** Slurry is fed into a sealed digester maintained at **mesophilic (35 – 40 °C)** or **thermophilic (50 – 55 °C)** conditions for **20 – 40 days**. Microbial consortium converts organics to **raw biogas**.
3. **Biogas Upgradation:** Raw biogas is upgraded using:
   - **Water Scrubbing** (PSA / VSA): Dissolves CO₂ and H₂S in water under pressure.
   - **Amine Absorption:** MEA/MDEA solvents selectively absorb CO₂.
   - **Membrane Separation:** Polyimide hollow-fiber membranes separate CO₂.
   The product has **CH₄ purity > 95%**.
4. **Compression:** Multi-stage reciprocating compressor compresses the gas to **200 – 250 bar**.
5. **Storage & Dispensing:** Stored in high-pressure cascades and dispensed to CNG vehicles.

**SATAT (Sustainable Alternative Towards Affordable Transportation) Scheme (MoPNG, Govt. of India):**
- **Target:** Establish **5000 CBG plants** by **2025 – 2026**.
- **Total CBG production target:** **15 MMT (Million Metric Tonnes)** by 2030.
- Linked to the **Compressed Natural Gas (CNG)** transport segment and **Piped Natural Gas (PNG)** domestic supply.

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Given:**
- Raw biogas = 2000 Nm³/day
- Composition: CH₄ = 60%, CO₂ = 38%, H₂S = 1%, N₂ = 1%

**(i) Volume of Pure CH₄:**

$$V_{CH_4} = 2000 \times 0.60 = 1200 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{day}$$

This is the raw methane. After upgradation to 95% purity, the methane recovered depends on recovery efficiency. Assuming **95% methane recovery** (typical for PSA):

$$V_{CH_4,\text{upgraded}} = 1200 \times 0.95 = 1140 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{day of pure CH}_4$$

**(ii) Energy Content of CBG:**

$$E_{CBG} = 1140 \text{ Nm}^3 \times 35.8 \text{ MJ/Nm}^3 = 40,812 \text{ MJ/day}$$

$$E_{CBG} = \dfrac{40,812}{3600} = 11.337 \text{ MWh/day}$$

**(iii) Equivalent Petrol Volume:**

$$V_{\text{petrol}} = \dfrac{40,812 \text{ MJ}}{32 \text{ MJ/L}} = \boxed{1275.4 \text{ litres of petrol per day}}$$

This daily output, when scaled annually, can displace approximately **4.65 lakh litres/year** of petrol — a meaningful contribution to India's import substitution goal.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing LHV and HHV of methane** — LHV = 50 MJ/kg (35.8 MJ/Nm³), HHV = 55.5 MJ/kg (39.8 MJ/Nm³). For *engine calculations*, always use LHV.
> 2. **Moisture content basis confusion** — "12% moisture" can be on a *dry basis (db)* or *wet basis (wb)*. Always re-confirm. (Dry basis: 12% db = 10.71% wb; Wet basis: 12% wb = 13.64% db).
> 3. **Methanogenesis pH cliff** — Students often write pH "between 5 and 8" for AD. The correct range is **6.8 – 7.4**; below 6.5, the digester sours.
> 4. **Stoichiometric ethanol yield** — Many students forget to convert from *moles to mass* and answer "2 moles" instead of "92 g per 180 g glucose".
> 5. **Equivalence Ratio inversion** — Some students define ER inversely (1/ER). The KTU convention is **ER = Actual/Stoichiometric**, with ER < 1 for gasification.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Gasification** is a thermochemical, partial-oxidation process producing **syngas**; ER is held between **0.25 – 0.40**.
- [x] **Pyrolysis** is purely thermal decomposition in the **absence of oxygen**; "hot & fast" → bio-oil; "cool & slow" → char.
- [x] **Fermentation** uses **yeast** to convert sugars to ethanol with a **theoretical yield of 51.1%** by mass.
- [x] **Anaerobic Digestion** is a **four-stage microbial process** ending in **methanogenesis** — the most pH-sensitive stage.
- [x] **CBG** is upgraded biogas compressed to **200 bar** with **≥ 95% CH₄ purity** under India's **SATAT scheme**.
- [x] **LHV of methane = 35.8 MJ/Nm³** is a high-yield constant for KTU numericals.
- [x] **Cold Gas Efficiency (60 – 75%)** is the standard performance metric for biomass gasifiers.
- [x] **Syngas from air gasification** has low calorific value (**4 – 7 MJ/Nm³**) due to N₂ dilution; **O₂/steam gasification** yields higher CV (**10 – 20 MJ/Nm³**).
- [x] **Bio-methanation** is sensitive to **temperature, pH, C/N ratio (optimum 20 – 30), and hydraulic retention time (HRT 20 – 40 days)**.
- [x] **Compressed Biogas** serves as a direct substitute for **CNG** and is fully interchangeable with natural gas at the dispensing nozzle.
